title: Drift Behavior in a Bounded-Confidence Opinion Model with Media Influence
summary: People's opinions can change both from their interactions with each other and from their interactions with media sources. Bounded-confidence models (BCMs) of opinion dynamics provide one framework to study such dynamics. In a BCM, the nodes of a network are agents with continuous-valued opinions, and these agents interact with each other via the edges of the network. In this paper, we extend the original Deffuant--Weisbuch (DW) BCM by incorporating influence from two media sources -- one with a positive value and one with a negative value -- to capture the effects of a polarized media landscape. We show both numerically and analytically that our extended DW model exhibits drifting behavior in which a large cluster of opinions shifts toward one of the media agents. We analyze how the drift trajectory and speed depend on the model parameters, and we identify conditions in which drift is promoted or suppressed. Our results provide insight into how competing media sources can influence collective opinion formation in social systems.

processed.summary:
• Extends the classic **Deffuant-Weisbuch (DW)** Bounded-Confidence Model by introducing **polarized media influence** through two external agents (positive and negative).
• Identifies a unique **drifting behavior** where large clusters of continuous-valued opinions shift toward one media pole based on parameter variations.
• Utilizes **analytical and numerical simulations** to quantify how the speed and trajectory of opinion shifts are governed by confidence bounds and network edges.
• Provides a framework for understanding how **competing external influences** interact with internal agent dynamics to shape collective social sentiment.
key_results: Discovery of sustained drifting behavior in opinion clusters under dual-media influence.
Analytical derivation of drift trajectory and speed as functions of model parameters.
Identification of specific 'confidence bounds' that either promote or suppress media-driven drift.
Evidence that media influence can prevent internal network consensus even when agents are highly connected.
Quantification of the relationship between media agent strength and the resulting shift in collective opinion.

title: Constraining primordial oscillations and inflationary particle production with Planck, ACT DR6, and DESI DR2
summary: Non-standard inflationary models often predict oscillatory features in the primordial power spectrum. We present constraints on general oscillatory templates for primordial power spectra, including those that vary linearly and logarithmically with wavenumber, as well as oscillations induced by inflationary particle production. We utilize the Planck 2018 and Atacama Cosmology Telescope Data Release 6 cosmic microwave background data as well as large-scale structure data from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument Data Release 2. To efficiently explore the multimodal posteriors for these models as well as performing mode comparisons, we integrate the preconditioned sequential Monte Carlo sampler, pocoMC, into the widely used sampling code, Cobaya. We find that the combined dataset tightens the 95% CL upper bounds on the general oscillation amplitudes to $A_{\text{lin}} < 0.021$, $A_{\text{log}} < 0.022$, and $A_{\text{log rf}} < 0.023$, restricting the amplitude to $\sim 2\%\, A_s$. For the inflationary particle production model, our analysis places a maximum a posteriori constraint on the coupling constant of $g = 0.034$. While these models all provide an improved fit to the data compared to the concordance $Λ$CDM model, the Bayesian evidence still reveals a moderate preference for $Λ$CDM compared to models with general oscillations and is inconclusive regarding the particle production model, suggesting that the added complexity of these models beyond the standard model is not statistically justified by current data.

processed.summary:
- Integrates the **pocoMC** (preconditioned sequential Monte Carlo) sampler into the **Cobaya** framework to efficiently navigate high-dimensional, multimodal posterior distributions.
- Analyzes combined observational data from **Planck 2018**, **ACT DR6**, and **DESI DR2** to test non-standard inflationary physics.
- Evaluates the statistical validity of **primordial oscillations** and **inflationary particle production** against the standard Lambda-CDM model.
- Focuses on **Bayesian evidence** as the primary metric for model comparison and selection in complex cosmological parameter spaces.
key_results: Tightened 95% confidence level upper bounds on general oscillation amplitudes to less than 2.3% of the primordial power spectrum.
Established a maximum a posteriori constraint on the coupling constant g at 0.034 for particle production models.
Determined that while oscillatory models provide a better fit, Bayesian evidence still favors the simpler Lambda-CDM model.
Demonstrated that the added complexity of inflationary particle production is not yet statistically justified by current data.
Validated the preconditioned sequential Monte Carlo approach for handling complex, non-Gaussian likelihoods in cosmology.

title: Equilibrium as a Limit: The Competitive Canon Nested in an Adaptive, Information-Theoretic Economy
summary: The competitive equilibrium of general equilibrium theory exists as a fixed point and is, by the theorys own results on aggregate excess demand, in general silent on whether that fixed point is unique, stable, or attained. This paper takes the economy to be not a configuration to be solved for but a process to be recovered, an asymptotically mean stationary information source carrying a partially identified operator of statistical dependence, populated by agents that are finite-capacity information channels. Within this adaptive order the competitive, rational expectations equilibrium is recovered exactly, as a joint limit taken along an explicit scaling path. Three parameter limits and two fixed-point conditions deliver it, the entropy rate falls to zero, agent channel capacity diverges, selection intensity grows infinitely sharp, adaptive learning reaches its expectationally stable rest point, and the recovered structure ceases to coevolve. At that corner the limiting object satisfies the axioms of the canon and its rest state is a Walrasian equilibrium, away from it the adaptive economy is a strict generalisation, carrying a positive entropy rate and a recovered dependence structure that the equilibrium primitive cannot express. We give the nesting as a theorem, establish the result by result correspondence with existence, with the Sonnenschein Mantel Debreu indeterminacy, and with the regular economies recovery, and characterise exactly what the equilibrium limit erases.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a novel **Information-Theoretic Economy** framework where agents are modeled as **finite-capacity information channels** rather than static optimizers.
• Defines the classical **Competitive Equilibrium** as a specific limit case where **Entropy Rates** fall to zero and selection intensity becomes infinitely sharp.
• Introduces an **Adaptive Order** that generalizes Walrasian equilibrium, allowing for the recovery of statistical dependence structures that standard models cannot express.
• Establishes a rigorous mathematical nesting that reconciles **Adaptive Learning** with the Sonnenschein-Mantel-Debreu indeterminacy problem.
key_results: Recovery of the rational expectations equilibrium as a joint limit along an explicit scaling path.
Demonstration that zero-entropy states correspond exactly to the axioms of the competitive canon.
Identification of finite channel capacity as the primary constraint preventing traditional equilibrium in real-world systems.
Characterization of the 'erased' information structure when moving from adaptive processes to fixed-point solutions.
Proof that the adaptive economy acts as a strict generalization of the Walrasian model when entropy is positive.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers derive economic equilibrium as a zero-entropy limit of an adaptive, information-theoretic process involving finite-capacity agents.

title: Democratic ICAI: Debating Our Way to Steering Principles from Preferences
summary: Preference-based alignment often struggles to capture the reasoning that underlies human judgments. Many evaluations rely on multiple interacting criteria, yet pairwise labels reveal only the final choice rather than the considerations that shape preferences. Inverse Constitutional AI (ICAI) improves interpretability in decision making by summarizing preferences into natural-language principles, but its single-pass explanations miss much of the nuance involved in complex decisions. We introduce Democratic ICAI, a novel approach that gathers multiple competing rationales through structured persona debate, offering a broader and more expressive account of the factors influencing each comparison. From these richer signals, we derive clearer and more comprehensive steering principles and use them to guide decision modeling through both LLM-based and decision-tree judges. Experiments on creative preference benchmarks, MuCE-Pref and LiTBench, across multiple creative task categories show that Democratic ICAI yields a more faithful preference structure. It improves average preference prediction across tasks relative to deliberative prompting and principle-based baselines, while producing constitutions that LLM annotators prefer.

* Introduces **Democratic ICAI**, an enhancement of Inverse Constitutional AI that utilizes **structured persona debate** to move beyond binary preference labels toward nuanced underlying rationales.
* Implements a framework that distills multiple competing perspectives into a set of **steering principles**, creating a more comprehensive and interpretable account of human decision-making.
* Utilizes derived principles to guide **decision-tree judges** and LLM-based evaluators, ensuring preference models remain faithful to the original human intent.
* Validates the approach on creative benchmarks, demonstrating that **multi-agent deliberation** significantly improves the faithfulness of preference structures.
key_results: Democratic ICAI outperforms baseline ICAI and deliberative prompting in average preference prediction accuracy.
The method produces constitutions that are qualitatively preferred by LLM annotators over single-pass alternatives.

title: Agentic Hardware Design as Repository-Level Code Evolution
summary: We present HORIZON, a self-evolving agent framework that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution. A Markdown harness is compiled into a project pack containing domain knowledge, an executable evaluator, an acceptance predicate, and a git/runtime policy; a hands-free agent loop then evolves an isolated git worktree, using repository operations for state management, tracing, and replay. This extends prior works of repository-scale self-evolution from EDA software systems, to hardware-design artifacts themselves. We evaluate our approach on ChipBench, RTLLM, Verilog-Eval, and nine CVDP categories, achieving 100\% benchmark completion across all suites with a fully hands-free agentic loop. However, we do not claim that agentic AI for hardware design is solved: these benchmarks are controlled proxies for a much broader engineering problem in chip design. Section~\ref{sec:discuss} examines the limitations of the current study and highlights open research challenges.

* Leverages isolated **git worktrees** to enable high-fidelity tracing, replay, and iterative evolution of complex hardware design artifacts.
* Bridges the gap between **EDA software systems** and hardware-design artifacts through a unified agentic loop.
key_results: Achieved 100% completion rate across ChipBench, RTLLM, and Verilog-Eval benchmarks.

title: Parameter Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT) for Network Traffic Prediction via Dynamic Urban Congestion Integration
summary: Accurate network traffic prediction is a critical element for efficient resource allocation in dynamic urban cellular networks. However, prediction remains challenging because network demand is influenced by complex mobility patterns, congestion dynamics, and heterogeneous user behavior. This paper introduces the Parameter-Efficient Hybrid Transformer (PEHT), a network traffic prediction framework that integrates urban mobility and congestion information into a Transformer-based architecture. PEHT separates primary network communication features from secondary urban mobility features and incorporates Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) into the Transformer encoder to reduce the number of trainable parameters while maintaining high predictive accuracy. A multimodal fusion strategy then injects external mobility and congestion features into the decoder to improve traffic forecasting. Experiments on the Telecom Italia Milan dataset and multiple synthetic congestion scenarios show that PEHT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of RMSE, MAE, and $R^2$. The implementation is available in the GitHub repository.

- Introduces **PEHT**, a Transformer-based framework designed for cellular network traffic prediction by integrating **urban mobility** and congestion data.
- Utilizes **Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)** within the Transformer encoder to minimize trainable parameters while preserving predictive performance.
- Employs a **multimodal fusion strategy** in the decoder to effectively merge primary network features with secondary urban congestion indicators.
- Optimizes **resource allocation** in dynamic urban environments by capturing complex mobility patterns and heterogeneous user behaviors.
key_results: Significantly reduces trainable parameters compared to full Transformer models using LoRA.
Achieved superior performance in RMSE and MAE metrics on the Telecom Italia Milan dataset.

title: Learning Topology-Aware Representations via Test-Time Adaptation for Anomaly Segmentation
summary: Test-time adaptation (TTA) has emerged as a promising paradigm for mitigating distribution shifts in deep models. However, existing TTA approaches for anomaly segmentation remain limited by their reliance on pixel-level heuristics, such as confidence thresholding or entropy minimisation, which fail to preserve structural consistency under noise and texture variation. Moreover, they typically treat anomaly maps as flat intensity fields, ignoring the higher-order spatial relationships that characterise complex defect geometries. We introduce TopoTTA (Topological Test-Time Adaptation), a novel framework that integrates persistent homology, a tool from topological data analysis, into the TTA pipeline to enforce geometric and structural coherence during adaptation. By applying multi-level cubical complex filtration to anomaly score maps, TopoTTA derives robust topological pseudo-labels that guide a lightweight test-time classifier, enhancing segmentation quality without retraining the backbone model. The approach avoids reliance on method-specific raw-score thresholding for mask binarisation, preserves connectivity, and generalises across both 2D and 3D modalities. Extensive experiments across six standard benchmarks (MVTec AD, VisA, Real-IAD, MVTec 3D-AD, AnomalyShapeNet, and MVTec LOCO) demonstrate an average 15% F1 improvement over state-of-the-art unsupervised anomaly detection and segmentation methods, with the largest gains on anomalies exhibiting complex geometric or structural variations. These findings suggest that integrating topological reasoning into test-time adaptation provides a principled route to structure-aware generalisation, bridging the gap between geometric learning and robust adaptation.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **TopoTTA**, a framework that integrates **persistent homology** and **topological data analysis (TDA)** into the test-time adaptation (TTA) pipeline to maintain structural consistency.
• Employs **multi-level cubical complex filtration** on anomaly score maps to generate robust **topological pseudo-labels** for guidance.
• Implements a **lightweight test-time classifier** that adapts to specific test instances without requiring a full retraining of the backbone model.
• Bridges the gap between **geometric learning** and robust adaptation by replacing pixel-level heuristics with higher-order spatial relationship reasoning.
key_results: Average 15% F1 score improvement over state-of-the-art unsupervised anomaly detection methods.
Superior performance on benchmarks including MVTec AD, VisA, Real-IAD, and MVTec 3D-AD.
Effective preservation of connectivity and geometric coherence in complex defect masks.
Successfully generalized across both 2D image data and 3D modality benchmarks.
Eliminated reliance on method-specific raw-score thresholding for binarization via topological reasoning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce TopoTTA, using persistent homology to improve anomaly segmentation via structure-aware test-time adaptation.

title: Graphon Mean Field Game of mutual holding
summary: This paper studies the mean field game of mutual holding proposed by Djete and Touzi(AAP, 2024), and consider the case where the interactions among agents are described by a graphon. We adopt the formulation on the enlarged space which is modeled using the joint law of the value process and the graphon label, as in Lacker and Soret(MOR, 2023). Under suitable conditions on the graphon function, we are able to provide the explicit characterization of the optimal strategy, prove the wellposedness of associated Mckean-Vlasov SDE and establish the convergence results of the Nash equilibria. The key technique consists in a detailed analysis of the continuity property under the $\mathcal{WOP}_2$ metric, and tailor-made arguments for different graphon equilibria under different regularities of the model.

processed.summary:
• Extends the **Graphon Mean Field Game (GMFG)** framework to model mutual holding scenarios within large-scale agent networks.
• Utilizes an **enlarged space formulation** based on the joint law of the value process and graphon labels to handle agent heterogeneity.
• Formally proves the **well-posedness** of the associated McKean-Vlasov Stochastic Differential Equations (SDEs).
• Analyzes the **convergence of Nash equilibria** from finite-player games to the graphon limit using the WOP2 metric.
key_results: Explicit characterization of the optimal strategy for agents in a mutual holding model.
Verification of existence and uniqueness (well-posedness) for the McKean-Vlasov SDE.
Rigorous proof of the convergence of Nash equilibria in large populations to the graphon limit.
Application of the WOP2 metric to analyze continuity properties in complex state spaces.
Development of stability results for different graphon equilibria under various model regularities.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers establish the existence and convergence of Nash equilibria in mutual holding games using graphon mean field theory.

title: Parameter-Efficient Continuous-Variable Photonic Quantum Neural Networks for Edge Quantum AI: Demonstration in Oral Cancer Detection
summary: Early detection of oral cancer markedly improves clinical outcomes, yet specialized diagnostic tools remain scarce in low-resource settings. Smartphone-based screening is a scalable alternative but needs lightweight models that run within edge-hardware constraints. Hybrid classical-quantum architectures are emerging candidates for parameter-efficient learning, yet most rely on qubit hardware that needs cryogenic operation, unsuitable for edge deployment. Continuous-variable (CV) photonic quantum computing, which operates at room temperature, offers a complementary route. We investigate a hybrid classical-CV quantum classifier for oral cancer detection from smartphone images. The pipeline combines a MobileNetV1 feature extractor, principal component analysis to 16 dimensions, and a parameterized CV-QNN of displacement, interferometric, and Kerr gates on a photonic backend. We propose a simplified $Φ\circ D \circ U_1$ CV-QNN architecture that cuts trainable parameters 40-45% relative to the standard CV-QNN layer of Killoran et al. (2019a), and identify dimensionality-reduction and encoding-restriction strategies that mitigate barren plateaus, raising loss-gradient variance by roughly 58 orders of magnitude. Whether the simplified layer beats the full layer is width-dependent: the full layer holds a small but significant edge at two qumodes, whereas the simplified layer is significantly better at four qumodes using 44% fewer parameters. The strongest model, a four-qumode simplified CV-QNN with only 18 parameters, attains the highest validation AUC of all models, exceeds a 55-parameter classical baseline using 67% fewer parameters, and reaches 100% calibrated test accuracy across all seeds. These results support CV photonic quantum machine learning for parameter-efficient, room-temperature medical image classification and motivate progress toward edge quantum AI.

- Proposes a **hybrid classical-quantum architecture** utilizing **Continuous-Variable (CV) photonic computing**, which allows for room-temperature operation suitable for **edge-hardware deployment**.
- Introduces a simplified **CV-QNN layer** that reduces trainable parameters by **40-45%** compared to standard photonic architectures while maintaining or improving performance.
- Implements a pipeline combining **MobileNetV1**, **PCA dimensionality reduction**, and a specialized photonic backend to classify medical images with extreme **parameter efficiency**.
- Mitigates the **barren plateau problem** in quantum circuits, increasing loss-gradient variance by approximately **58 orders of magnitude** through strategic encoding and dimensionality constraints.
key_results: Simplified 4-qumode CV-QNN achieved the highest validation AUC using only 18 parameters.
The quantum model outperformed a 55-parameter classical baseline with 67% fewer parameters.
Reached 100% calibrated test accuracy for oral cancer detection across all experimental seeds.

Demonstrated successful room-temperature quantum machine learning for medical imaging without cryogenic requirements.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate a parameter-efficient photonic quantum network that outperforms classical models for room-temperature edge medical diagnostics.

title: HPRO: Hierarchical Progressive Reward Optimization via Preference Extraction for Emotional Text-to-Speech
summary: Recently, Large Language Model (LLM)-based Text-to-Speech (TTS) models have achieved remarkable naturalness. However, the standard Supervised Fine-Tuning paradigm often converges to statistically averaged prosody, limiting emotional expressiveness. While preference-driven optimization offers a promising alternative, existing approaches suffer from two structural mismatches: information conflict, where content and emotion in a shared latent space produce conflicting gradients, leading to reward hacking and semantic degradation; and scale gap, where sparse sentence-level rewards struggle to guide dense frame-level generation. To overcome these challenges, we propose HPRO, a hierarchical progressive reward optimization framework. Within HPRO, we introduce the HD-Emo codec as a novel differentiable reward model to resolve the information conflict. It extracts speech into distinct content and style preference tokens, structurally isolating emotional optimization from semantic content. Building upon this structured preference space, HPRO bridges the scale gap by progressively aligning frame-, word- and sentence-level objectives. Experiments demonstrate that HPRO significantly enhances emotional expressiveness, while effectively preserving linguistic intelligibility. The code and audio samples are publicly available at https://xxh333.github.io/hpro-demo/.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **HPRO (Hierarchical Progressive Reward Optimization)**, a framework designed to enhance emotional expressiveness in LLM-based TTS models by addressing prosody averaging.
- Introduces the **HD-Emo codec**, a novel differentiable reward model that structurally isolates speech into distinct **content and style preference tokens**.
- Resolves the **information conflict** and **scale gap** issues by progressively aligning objectives at the frame, word, and sentence levels.
- Achieves a significant boost in **emotional prosody** and linguistic intelligibility by preventing reward hacking and semantic degradation.
key_results: Successfully decoupled emotional style from semantic content using structured preference tokens.
Mitigated 'reward hacking' and semantic degradation commonly found in joint latent space optimizations.
Bridged the scale gap between sparse sentence-level rewards and dense frame-level speech generation.
Demonstrated superior emotional expressiveness compared to standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT).
Maintained high linguistic intelligibility while significantly improving the variety of emotional prosody.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: HPRO researchers introduce a hierarchical reward optimization framework to disentangle emotional style from semantic content in TTS synthesis.

title: How Width and Data Shape Generalization Scaling Laws in Quadratic Neural Networks
summary: Understanding how performance scales jointly with model size and data is a central problem in modern machine learning. Existing theoretical works on scaling laws typically describe generalization as a function of data or compute, often in fixed-feature or infinite-width regimes and for online SGD. Here, we instead study how generalization scales with the number of trainable parameters and the number of samples in a feature-learning model. We analyze $\ell_2$-regularized empirical test error minimization in a quadratic two-layer network in a finite-sample setting with structured data. This setting allows for an explicit characterization of the generalization error as a function of the number of samples, model width, and regularization. Our results reveal a phase diagram with distinct scaling regimes as the number of parameters varies. In particular, the generalization error follows data-dependent power laws controlled by the spectral structure of the target. We further characterize the transitions between regimes, including the onset of interpolation, and their impact on generalization.

processed.summary:
- Investigates the joint scaling behavior of **model width** and **sample size** in quadratic two-layer neural networks.
- Transitions from infinite-width or fixed-feature assumptions to analyze **feature-learning** in finite-sample settings.
- Establishes a **phase diagram** identifying distinct generalization regimes based on the ratio of parameters to data.
- Links **generalization error** to data-dependent power laws influenced by the spectral structure of the target function.
key_results: Defined an explicit characterization of generalization error as a function of samples, width, and regularization.
Identified a phase diagram where scaling regimes shift based on the number of trainable parameters.
Found that generalization power laws are fundamentally controlled by the target's spectral structure.
Mapped the onset of the interpolation regime and its specific consequences for test error.
Proved that feature-learning models exhibit scaling trajectories distinct from traditional fixed-feature models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that neural network scaling laws are jointly determined by model width and the target's spectral structure.

title: Entropy density functional theory for inhomogeneous fluids
summary: We present an exact variational scheme for the physics of inhomogeneous classical fluids in thermal equilibrium. A joint metadensity minimization principle is proven for the one-body density and the global interparticle distance distribution. The theory bypasses the inhomogeneous two-body density and thus remains computationally simple. A universal excess entropy functional accounts for all many-body correlations in arbitrary pairwise interacting systems. The framework is relevant for neural functional machine learning, for soft matter design, and for predicting structural correlation functions via entropic test-particle and meta-Ornstein-Zernike routes.

processed.summary:
• Proposes an exact **variational scheme** for the physics of inhomogeneous classical fluids using a joint metadensity minimization principle.
• Introduces a **universal excess entropy functional** that accounts for many-body correlations while bypassing computationally expensive two-body densities.
• Establishes a theoretical foundation for **neural functional machine learning** in soft matter design and structural correlation prediction.

Development of a universal excess entropy functional for arbitrary pairwise interacting systems.
Elimination of the need for inhomogeneous two-body density, simplifying computational requirements.
Establishment of entropic test-particle and meta-Ornstein-Zernike routes for structural analysis.
Demonstration of relevance for neural functional machine learning in physical system simulations.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers present an exact variational entropy theory to accelerate neural functional machine learning for inhomogeneous fluid simulations.

title: Unleashing Infinite Motion: Scaling Expressive Quadrupedal Motion via Generative Video Priors
summary: Quadruped robots have achieved remarkable locomotion, yet their behavioral repertoire remains confined to a few gaits--far from the expressive, companion-like presence long envisioned for them. Attempts to import the humanoid recipe of large-scale motion data have inherited one tacit assumption: that robot motion must first pass through an animal body, making data collection dependent on cooperative animals, reconstruction fragile across species, and retargeting ill-posed across incompatible morphologies. We propose Uni-Mo, a fully automated pipeline that removes the animal from the loop by reframing data scarcity as a generation problem: an LLM proposes motion prompts, a video diffusion model synthesizes the corresponding robot behaviors, and the generated videos are lifted into 3D reference trajectories used to train tracking policies deployed on a real Unitree Go2. To make naively-drifting generations reliably extractable, we introduce an Identity Consistency Loss that enforces appearance coherence across frames. We release Quad-Imaginarium at https://github.com/GaoLii/Quad-Imaginarium.git, the resulting open-source dataset of 7,488 language-annotated quadruped motions (18.5 hours) spanning acrobatic and performative behaviors. We validate 392 randomly sampled motions on a real Unitree Go2 with a 96.7% deployment success rate, complemented by a 97.6% success rate across the full dataset in simulation.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **Uni-Mo**, a fully automated pipeline that generates robot locomotion data by using **video diffusion models** as generative priors for motion synthesis.
- Utilizes an **LLM** to generate diverse behavioral prompts, which are converted into videos and then lifted into **3D reference trajectories** for policy training.
- Introduces an **Identity Consistency Loss** to maintain visual coherence across generated frames, ensuring stable motion extraction for **Reinforcement Learning**.
- Bridges the gap between **Generative AI** and physical robotics by deploying expressive, acrobatic behaviors directly onto a **Unitree Go2** quadruped.
key_results: Creation of Quad-Imaginarium, an open-source dataset with 7,488 language-annotated motions.
Achieved 18.5 hours of diverse, synthetic quadrupedal behavioral data without animal demonstrations.
Validated 392 sampled motions on real hardware with a 96.7% deployment success rate.
Maintained a 97.6% success rate across the entire dataset when tested in simulation environments.
Successfully demonstrated complex acrobatic and performative behaviors previously difficult to capture via animal retargeting.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Gao et al. introduce Uni-Mo, leveraging video diffusion priors to automate expressive quadruped motion generation for real-world deployment.

title: Qudit extension of parameterized IQP circuits: A generative quantum machine learning approach to integer data
summary: Parameterized Instantaneous Quantum Polynomial (IQP) circuits have proven useful in quantum generative learning models, particularly for binary distributions. However, when applied to non-binary datasets, they exhibit notable limitations: mapping integer values into qubit-compatible binary representations often destroys the original metric structure of the data. In this paper we aim to extend them to a qudits formulation operating on an integer mapping of the data. The IQP quantum circuit is adapted to encode each integer valued pixel into a bit-string of fixed length and quantum gates are transformed to follow the qudit formalism. As a generative machine learning approach, a suitable loss function for the circuit training and the calculation of the covariance matrix among features are developed and validated on the energy deposits from single-particle electron showers in the electromagnetic calorimeter of the CLIC detector. The method proposed in this work can be also extended to other applications that utilize quantum generative machine learning for non-binary data.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **qudit-based Parameterized Instantaneous Quantum Polynomial (IQP)** circuit architecture designed to handle integer data without the loss of metric structure inherent in binary mappings.
- Adapts quantum gates to the **qudit formalism**, encoding integer-valued pixels into fixed-length strings to maintain the relationship between numerical values.
- Introduces a specialized **generative loss function** and a methodology for calculating feature covariance matrices within the qudit framework.
- Validates the approach using high-energy physics data, specifically **single-particle electron showers** in the CLIC detector's electromagnetic calorimeter.
key_results: Successfully extended IQP circuits from traditional binary qubits to multi-state qudit systems.
Eliminated data distortion caused by mapping integer values to standard qubit-compatible binary representations.
Developed a training-ready qudit generative model capable of reproducing complex feature distributions.
Validated the circuit's ability to model energy deposits in a particle physics detector environment.
Established a scalable framework for applying quantum generative machine learning to non-binary datasets.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: CERN and CLIC researchers extend IQP circuits to qudits to preserve integer metric structures in quantum generative models.

title: Govern the Repository, Not the Agent: Measuring Ecosystem-Level Risk in AI-Native Software
summary: Autonomous coding agents now open and merge pull requests in shared repositories at scale, and the field evaluates them the way it has always evaluated components, one agent at a time, on isolated benchmark tasks. Yet agents that each pass their own tests still leave repositories that accumulate problems no single contribution accounts for. We ask whether this problem belongs to the individual agent or to the repository where it accumulates. We study integration friction, the cost of integrating a contribution into a codebase that other contributors are concurrently changing. Across more than 930,000 agent-authored pull requests, we measure how much of the variation in friction stays with the repository after the contribution, its author, its size, and its agent are accounted for. About half does, and it survives full controls. In the same repositories, agent-authored contributions concentrate this repository-level friction roughly twice as much as human ones (intraclass correlation 0.30 versus 0.16), a gap that holds after controlling for codebase size, age, task shape, process maturity, and merge path. The risk is a property of the ecosystem, not the agent. AI-native software is therefore better measured and governed at the ecosystem level than one agent at a time.

processed.summary:
- Evaluates the impact of **LLM Agents** on software ecosystems by measuring **integration friction** across 930,000+ agent-authored pull requests.
- Shifts the focus from individual component benchmarks to **ecosystem-level risk**, identifying how cumulative changes lead to repository decay.
- Utilizes **intraclass correlation** to prove that agents concentrate repository-level friction twice as much as human contributors (0.30 vs 0.16).
- Recommends a transition toward **repository governance** rather than isolated agent testing to manage the risks of AI-native software development.
key_results: Approximately 50% of integration friction variation is tied to the repository rather than the specific agent.
Agent-authored contributions concentrate repository-level friction roughly twice as much as human-authored ones.
The friction gap persists regardless of codebase size, age, task shape, or process maturity.
Isolated benchmark performance does not account for the cumulative architectural problems agents introduce.
Findings suggest that AI-native software risk is a property of the ecosystem, not the individual agent.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that AI agents increase repository-level integration friction, requiring ecosystem-level governance rather than individual component testing.

title: Humanizing Automatically Generated Unit Test Suites with LLM-Based Refactoring
summary: Search-based test generation tools such as EvoSuite produce compilable and high-coverage unit tests at scale, but their suites are often hard to read and maintain. LLMs can generate more natural tests, yet direct generation remains brittle, with compilation rates of only 51-78% in our study. We introduce TestHumanizer, a hybrid SBST+LLM approach that uses LLMs as controlled refactoring layers over compilable SBST suites to improve naming, structure, and developer-oriented clarity while preserving behavior and compilation validity. We evaluate TestHumanizer on 350 classes from Defects4J and SF110. EvoSuite generates 15 suites per class, and each suite is refactored under three context configurations using gpt-4o and mistral-large-2407, yielding 31,500 refactorings. TestHumanizer reaches 88-98% compilation rates, close to EvoSuite's 100% baseline and clearly above direct LLM generation. Structural coverage is largely preserved, typically within 1-2 percentage points, and 86-95% of refactorings satisfy a composite faithful-refactoring threshold. Refactored suites also improve predicted readability, reduce control-flow and cognitive complexity, and mitigate structural smells. The summary-based setting offers the most robust trade-off, while long code-centric prompts are more prone to hallucination-induced failures. A developer study on 30 classes and 444 test methods confirms significant gains in perceived readability and willingness to adopt, with Wilcoxon p less than 0.01 and substantial inter-rater agreement. Overall, LLMs are most effective not as standalone generators but as validation-gated refinement layers over robust SBST outputs.

processed.summary:
* **TestHumanizer** introduces a hybrid approach that integrates **Search-Based Software Testing (SBST)** with Large Language Models, using LLMs as a **controlled refactoring layer** to improve the readability of automatically generated suites.
* The architecture utilizes a **validation-gated refinement process**, transforming high-coverage but structurally complex EvoSuite outputs into developer-friendly code using models like **GPT-4o** and **Mistral-Large**.
* This methodology effectively decouples the logic generation (handled by SBST) from the syntactic and stylistic presentation (handled by the LLM), ensuring **behavioral preservation** and high compilation validity.
key_results: Refactored suites achieved 88-98% compilation rates, significantly higher than the 51-78% seen in direct LLM generation.
Structural coverage remained stable, typically staying within 1-2 percentage points of the SBST baseline.
86-95% of generated refactorings satisfied strict criteria for behavioral faithfulness and correctness.
Significant improvements were measured in predicted readability and reductions in cognitive and control-flow complexity.
Developer studies confirmed a high willingness to adopt refactored suites, supported by a Wilcoxon p-value less than 0.01.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that LLMs excel as validation-gated refactoring layers for SBST-generated tests, maximizing both coverage and developer readability.

title: Towards Value-Constrained Credit Assignment in Fully Delegated AI Cooperatives
summary: We propose a framework for reward allocation in fully delegated AI cooperatives where humans are represented by agents that contribute data and participate in model updates under heterogeneous value constraints. The key idea is to credit only those updates that remain admissible after screening them against each principal's value profile. We formulate value-conditioned gradient filtering, online marginal contribution signals, and cumulative revenue settlement within a traversal learning (TL) substrate. TL is especially attractive here because it performs decentralized backpropagation without the quality loss associated with aggregation-centric distributed learning and, we argue, offers a finer attribution substrate than FedAvg-style federated learning by preserving explicit traversal and gradient paths. The framework is positioned against data valuation, federated contribution estimation, personalized federated learning, and pluralistic alignment.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a framework for **fully delegated AI cooperatives** where human-representative agents contribute data under heterogeneous value constraints.
- Introduces **Traversal Learning (TL)** to perform decentralized backpropagation, preserving explicit gradient paths for more granular attribution than **FedAvg**-style federated learning.
- Implements **value-conditioned gradient filtering** to ensure that model updates are only credited if they align with a principal's specific value profile.
- Links decentralized model updates to **online marginal contribution signals** for automated and fair cumulative revenue settlement.
key_results: Formalized a mechanism for value-conditioned gradient filtering to screen updates against principal constraints.
Demonstrated that Traversal Learning preserves explicit gradient paths, enabling superior credit attribution over traditional aggregation methods.
Developed a method for calculating online marginal contribution signals during the training traversal.
Integrated pluralistic alignment goals with a decentralized economic revenue settlement framework.
Showed that TL avoids the quality degradation typically associated with aggregation-centric distributed learning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose Traversal Learning to enable value-aligned credit assignment and revenue settlement in decentralized AI cooperatives.

title: HAT-4D: Lifting Monocular Video for 4D Multi-Object Interactions via Human-Agent Collaboration
summary: Extracting dynamic 4D object interactions from massive, in-the-wild monocular videos offers a highly efficient data collection pathway for scaling Embodied AI and training VLAs. However, existing monocular 4D reconstruction methods primarily focus on isolated objects, often failing under the severe occlusions and complex dynamics inherent in multi-object interactions. To bridge this gap, we propose HAT-4D, the first agentic framework designed to reconstruct the 3D geometry, temporal dynamics, and physical interactions of multiple objects from a single video. By integrating VLMs with a multi-level human-in-the-loop feedback mechanism, HAT-4D efficiently resolves depth ambiguities and interaction-induced occlusions during 3D generation and 4D propagation, yielding physically plausible assets without relying on expensive multicamera rigs. As a scalable data engine, HAT-4D facilitates the creation of MVOIK-4D, an open-world benchmark for monocular 4D interaction reconstruction, accompanied by a novel multi-dimensional evaluation protocol focused on physical plausibility and temporal consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HAT-4D achieves SOTA performance on most evaluation metrics, while maintaining competitive semantic alignment. Ablation studies show that introducing a small amount of human feedback improves interaction reconstruction. Moreover, the data produced by HAT-4D effectively improves baseline performance when used for fine-tuning. Our data and code are available at https://lijiaxin0111.github.io/HAT4D/

processed.summary:
• HAT-4D introduces an **agentic framework** for lifting monocular video into 4D, focusing specifically on the **3D geometry, temporal dynamics, and physical interactions** of multiple objects.
• The architecture integrates **Vision-Language Models (VLMs)** with a multi-level **human-in-the-loop (HITL)** feedback mechanism to resolve depth ambiguities and interaction-induced occlusions.
• It serves as a scalable **data engine** to generate high-quality synthetic data for training **Vision-Language-Action (VLA)** models and Embodied AI systems.
• The framework includes the **MVOIK-4D benchmark**, providing a novel protocol for evaluating physical plausibility and temporal consistency in open-world settings.
key_results: Achieved State-of-the-Art (SOTA) performance in multi-object 4D interaction reconstruction metrics.
Successfully resolved depth and occlusion challenges without requiring expensive multi-camera rigs.
Validated that minimal human feedback significantly enhances the accuracy of interaction reconstruction.
Established MVOIK-4D as a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating physical plausibility in 4D generation.
Demonstrated that HAT-4D generated data effectively improves baseline performance when used for model fine-tuning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Jiaxin Li et al. introduce HAT-4D, an agentic framework using human-agent collaboration to reconstruct complex 4D multi-object interactions from monocular video.

title: Non-primary square roots in massive gravity
summary: Non-linear dRGT massive and bimetric gravities are complicated theories constructed in terms of square roots of matrices. Apart from the technical issues of successfully working with such square roots, there is also a problem of their non-uniqueness. There are claims in the literature that one should better use the principal root. This is a very reasonable conclusion. However, the motivation they give for it is that otherwise there would be non-primary square roots violating the general covariance. In this paper, I would like to show that, if properly understood, the non-primary square roots are also perfectly covariant. At the same time, I recall the relatively old observation that the real problem with such square roots lies in perturbation theory around them. In terms of matrices, it simply does not exist. In terms of the elementary symmetric polynomials used in the Lagrangian density, it is not analytic. Moreover, the non-principal square roots are more prone to getting into the complex domain.

processed.summary:
* Investigates the mathematical properties of **non-primary square roots** of matrices within **dRGT massive and bimetric gravity** theories.
* Argues that **non-primary roots** are **perfectly covariant**, refuting previous claims that only principal roots maintain general covariance.
* Identifies a critical failure in **perturbation theory** around non-principal roots, characterizing the resulting Lagrangian as non-analytic.
key_results: Proof that non-primary square roots maintain general covariance in gravity theories.
Identification of the non-existence of matrix-level perturbation theory for non-primary roots.
Determination that elementary symmetric polynomials are non-analytic for non-principal roots.
Increased risk of non-principal roots transitioning into the complex domain.
Validation of the principal root preference based on analyticity rather than covariance.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv research demonstrates that non-primary square roots in massive gravity maintain covariance but fail due to non-analytic perturbation.

title: Non-Linear Strategic Classification Made Practical
summary: Algorithmic developments in Strategic Classification have been mostly limited to linear classifiers in settings where the best response has a closed-form solution or can be easily approximated. While some work has explored the role of non-linear classifiers in strategic settings, progress in this direction is impeded by the computational intractability of the strategic behaviour. Addressing this, we present a novel method for approximating the best response by exploiting Lagrangian duality. By reformulating the strategic response as a constrained optimisation problem, we can construct a Lagrangian that is amenable to first order optimisation methods. This approach reproduces closed-form strategic behaviour in linear settings and can be straight-forwardly applied to non-linear settings. We show how the Implicit Function Theorem can be used in conjunction with our proposed response formulation during classifier learning to compute the total gradient of the loss. This connects the classifier parameters directly to the consequent strategic behaviour, yielding a novel training algorithm that can exploit this relationship. Experimental evaluation shows that the resulting models achieve improved strategic accuracy on common machine learning datasets.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a novel method for **non-linear strategic classification** by approximating the agent's best response using **Lagrangian duality**.
- Formulates the strategic response as a **constrained optimization problem**, allowing it to be solved via first-order optimization methods instead of closed-form approximations.
- Integrates the **Implicit Function Theorem (IFT)** during training to compute the total gradient of the loss, linking classifier parameters to strategic behavior.
- Enables the practical application of **robust classification** in environments where users intentionally manipulate features to improve their predicted outcomes.
key_results: Successfully reproduces closed-form strategic behavior in linear settings using the new Lagrangian formulation.
Extends strategic classification capabilities to non-linear models like deep neural networks.
Introduces a differentiable training algorithm that accounts for how agents shift their features in response to model updates.
Demonstrates superior strategic accuracy compared to traditional baselines on common ML datasets.
Provides a computationally tractable path for solving previously 'intractable' strategic behavior in non-linear settings.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a Lagrangian-based optimization framework to enable practical, non-linear strategic classification for robust machine learning.

title: COCOLogic-V2: Identifying Logical Inconsistencies via Truly Hard-Negatives
summary: While interpretable models such as concept bottleneck models (CBMs) and program synthesis methods enable verification of model decisions, their evaluation is typically limited to simple tasks, leaving complex reasoning on real-world images largely unexplored. We introduce COCOLogic-V2, an object-centric dataset for visual inductive reasoning on real-world images covering a broad subset of first-order logic. By categorizing samples into positive variants, near-boundary (NB), and far-from-boundary (FB) negatives, COCOLogic-V2 enables fine-grained diagnosis of model accountability. Our evaluations show that models tend to separate positive and FB samples well but fail on NB samples, while perceptual noise and large rule-induced search spaces pose additional challenges in few-shot settings. Together, these results highlight that visual inductive reasoning remains an open challenge and COCOLogic-V2 provides a concrete foundation for advancing methods in this direction.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **COCOLogic-V2**, an object-centric dataset specifically designed for **visual inductive reasoning** using first-order logic on real-world imagery.
- Utilizes a unique categorization strategy comprising **positive variants**, **near-boundary (NB)** negatives, and **far-from-boundary (FB)** negatives to evaluate model accountability.
- Focuses on the limitations of **Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs)** and program synthesis when transitioning from toy datasets to complex, real-world visual logic tasks.
key_results: Models demonstrate high accuracy in separating positive samples from far-from-boundary negatives.
Current architectures consistently fail to identify near-boundary negatives, revealing logical inconsistencies.
Perceptual noise in real-world images significantly hampers logical reasoning performance.
Large rule-induced search spaces pose a critical challenge for models in few-shot reasoning scenarios.
The dataset highlights a significant gap between perceptual capability and complex visual inductive reasoning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce COCOLogic-V2 to expose model failures in visual inductive reasoning through challenging near-boundary logical negatives.

title: The Remittance Blueprint: Data-driven Intelligence for Sri Lanka
summary: This study analyzes Sri Lankan migration and remittances over 32 years (1994-2025). Using a 384-month harmonized dataset, we apply exploratory data analysis, stationarity corrected time-series modeling (ADF, Johansen, VAR/VECM), and supervised learning. Results reveal remittance inflows are primarily driven by external macroeconomic variables, specifically exchange rate dynamics and global oil prices, rather than domestic indicators. Impulse response analysis confirms the asymmetric impact of currency depreciation and oil price shocks. Predictively, multivariate machine learning models outperform traditional univariate approaches; Ridge Regression achieves a 73.8% accuracy improvement over SARIMA (Annualized RMSE: USD 494.8 Mn). The optimized framework projects 2026 remittances at USD 9,001 million under stable conditions. These findings highlight the structural dependence of remittances on global economies, emphasizing the need for robust exchange rate policies, skilled migration, and formal financial channels to enhance long-term economic resilience.

- Implements a **multivariate machine learning framework** to analyze a 384-month dataset (1994-2025) for remittance forecasting.
- Employs **stationarity corrected time-series modeling** including ADF, Johansen, and VAR/VECM to isolate external macroeconomic drivers.
- Replaces traditional univariate approaches with **Ridge Regression**, resulting in a 73.8% improvement in predictive accuracy.
- Evaluates the **asymmetric impact** of currency depreciation and oil price shocks on national economic resilience via impulse response analysis.
key_results: Ridge Regression achieved a 73.8% accuracy improvement over the traditional SARIMA model.
Global oil prices and exchange rate dynamics were identified as the primary drivers of remittance inflows.
Domestic indicators showed significantly less predictive power than external macroeconomic variables.
The model recorded an annualized RMSE of USD 494.8 million.
Remittances are projected to reach USD 9,001 million by 2026 under stable conditions.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that multivariate Ridge Regression significantly outperforms traditional time-series models for predicting national remittance inflows.

title: Cognitive Episodes in LLM Reasoning Traces Enable Interpretable Human Item Difficulty Prediction
summary: Predicting human item difficulty is central to educational assessment, where reliable estimates support fairness and effective test construction. Existing methods often depend on costly human calibration or item-level textual representations, providing limited evidence about the cognitive processes that make items difficult. We argue that difficulty should be viewed not only as a property of item text, but also as an observable consequence of the problem-solving burden an item induces. Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) offer scalable process evidence through reasoning traces, but such evidence must be structured to support interpretable modeling. To this end, we introduce Epi2Diff (Episode to Difficulty), a framework that maps LRM reasoning traces into cognitively grounded episode sequences. These episodes group trace segments into functional problem-solving states, enabling difficulty to be modeled through reasoning scale, effort allocation, and state transitions. Epi2Diff extracts compact episode-dynamic features and combines them with semantic item representations for human difficulty prediction. Experiments on four real-world human difficulty datasets show that Epi2Diff consistently outperforms strong baselines, including fine-tuned small language models, LLM in-context learning, and supervised LLM adaptation. On SAT-derived classification benchmarks, Epi2Diff achieves an 8.1% average relative gain over supervised LLM fine-tuning baselines. Further analyses show that harder items induce more effortful, iterative, and implementation-centered episode dynamics, rather than merely longer responses. These results demonstrate that cognitive episodes in LRM reasoning traces provide a predictive and interpretable process representation for human item difficulty, offering a new lens for educational measurement with reasoning models.

- Introduces **Epi2Diff** (Episode to Difficulty), a framework that maps **Large Reasoning Model (LRM)** traces into cognitively grounded episode sequences for human item difficulty prediction.
- Shifts the paradigm from item-level textual representations to observing the **problem-solving burden** induced by an item as reflected in structured reasoning dynamics.
- Utilizes **episode-dynamic features**, including effort allocation and state transitions, to provide a more interpretable and predictive representation of cognitive effort than simple response length.
- Integrates **semantic item representations** with process evidence to create a hybrid model for educational measurement and assessment.
key_results: Epi2Diff consistently outperforms fine-tuned SLMs, LLM in-context learning, and supervised adaptation.
Achieved an 8.1% average relative gain over supervised LLM fine-tuning on SAT-derived benchmarks.
Demonstrated that harder items induce more iterative and implementation-centered episode dynamics.
Proved that reasoning scale and state transitions are more predictive of difficulty than raw token count.
Validated the framework across four diverse real-world human difficulty datasets.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Epi2Diff, mapping LRM reasoning traces to cognitive episodes to accurately predict human problem-solving difficulty.

title: LLawCo: Learning Laws of Cooperation for Modeling Embodied Multi-Agent Behavior
summary: Embodied agents operating in decentralized and partially observable environments have attracted growing attention in recent years. However, existing large language model (LLM)-based agents often exhibit behaviors that are misaligned with their partners or inconsistent with the environment state, leading to inefficient cooperation and poor task success. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework, Learning Laws of Cooperation (LLawCo), that enables embodied agents to autonomously align with both their partners and task objectives. Our framework allows agents to reflect on past failures to extract misaligned behavioral patterns, which are used to derive high-level behavioral laws, such as "Talk when necessary" and "Wait for partner." These laws are explicitly incorporated into the agents' chains of thought via supervised fine-tuning, aligning their reasoning with task requirements and the behavior of other agents. To evaluate our approach, we introduce PARTNR-Dialog, a large-scale multi-agent communicative and cooperative planning benchmark built on the PARTNR environment. Experiments on existing tasks and our new benchmark demonstrate significant improvements in cooperative efficiency and task success rates. Across four backbone LLMs, our method achieves average success rate improvements of 4.5% on the PARTNR-Dialog benchmark and 6.8% on the TDW-MAT benchmark over state-of-the-art open-source communicative agent frameworks. See the LLawCo project page for details: https://www.merl.com/research/highlights/LLawCo

processed.summary:
* **LLawCo** introduces a novel framework for **embodied multi-agent cooperation** by teaching agents to derive and follow high-level behavioral laws.
* The architecture utilizes a reflection mechanism where agents analyze past failures to extract **misaligned patterns**, which are then distilled into explicit cooperative strategies.
* These laws are integrated into the agents' **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** via **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)**, ensuring reasoning aligns with both the partner's state and task objectives.
* The framework addresses challenges in **partially observable environments** by standardizing communicative behaviors like 'Talk when necessary' or 'Wait for partner.'
key_results: Introduction of PARTNR-Dialog, a large-scale benchmark for multi-agent communicative planning.
Achieved a 4.5% average success rate improvement on the PARTNR-Dialog benchmark.
Demonstrated a 6.8% success rate increase on the TDW-MAT benchmark over open-source SOTA.
Validated performance gains across four distinct backbone LLM architectures.
Proven effectiveness in reducing behavioral misalignment in decentralized, partially observable tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: MERL researchers introduce LLawCo to enhance multi-agent cooperation by fine-tuning LLMs with behavioral laws derived from past failures.

title: CPAgents: Agentic Composite Phenotype Generation for Cardiac Disease Association
summary: Identifying robust associations between cardiac imaging phenotypes and clinical diseases is fundamental to population-scale cardiovascular research and reliable risk stratification. However, current phenome-wide association studies rely on pre-defined, single-variable phenotypes or expert-crafted features, which limits their ability to capture clinically meaningful non-linear effects and cross-phenotype interactions. To address this, we propose CPAgents, an iterative phenotype-Composition framework for cardiovascular Phenome-wide association study (PheWAS) that automatically constructs and validates interpretable composite phenotypes (e.g., polynomial, ratio, and interaction forms) from base imaging features. Specifically, our system coordinates three agents: (i) an Analyst that identifies statistical pathologies and nominates candidate transformations; (ii) a Proposer that generates constrained, medically and statistically motivated expressions under numerical safety rules; and (iii) a Verifier that evaluates candidates using multi-stage criteria and produces transparent evidence trails for accepted phenotypes. Evaluated on a population-scale cardiac imaging cohort, the discovered composite phenotypes markedly improve disease discrimination: across 72 classifier-disease-metric combinations, our variants achieve the top rank in 56 cases versus 18 for baselines, with gains observed across all nine clinical disease categories. Our framework yields compact, clinically interpretable phenotype formulas with transparent evidence trails, enabling scalable discovery of stronger phenotype-disease associations beyond expert-driven feature selection.

processed.summary:
• **CPAgents** introduces an iterative, multi-agent framework designed to automate the discovery of **composite phenotypes** for cardiovascular research.
• The architecture utilizes a trio of specialized agents—**Analyst, Proposer, and Verifier**—to identify statistical gaps, propose safe mathematical transformations, and validate clinical relevance.
• By moving beyond single-variable features, the system captures **non-linear effects** and cross-phenotype interactions through interpretable expressions like ratios and polynomials.
• The framework ensures **model transparency** by producing human-readable evidence trails for every newly generated feature.
key_results: Achieved top performance in 56 out of 72 classifier-disease-metric combinations.
Demonstrated performance gains across all nine clinical disease categories analyzed.
Generated compact, medically motivated phenotype formulas that outperformed expert-crafted features.
Established a multi-stage verification process to ensure numerical safety and statistical validity.
Provided transparent evidence trails, enabling scalable discovery in population-scale cardiac imaging cohorts.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CPAgents, an agentic framework that automates composite phenotype discovery to enhance cardiac disease association modeling.

title: Vacuum Fluctuation-Induced State Switching in Degenerate Optical Parametric Oscillators
summary: Bistable driven-dissipative systems near bifurcations can exhibit noise-activated switching between steady states. Here, we investigate how quantum vacuum fluctuations induce such switching in a biased optical parametric oscillator (OPO), a nonlinear system with intrinsic bistability. We show how microscopic quantum fluctuations driving macroscopic transitions can be controlled with an external bias field that reshapes the OPO steady-state metapotential. We derive analytical expressions for the average switching time and validate them through simulations of the OPO field distribution and inter-state probability flow under bias injection. We further examine how switching depends on bias strength, pump gain, and optical nonlinearity. Our findings clarify how quantum noise can shape macroscopic dynamics and provide a foundation for noise-assisted photonic machine learning and probabilistic quantum gates.

processed.summary:
• Investigates the role of **microscopic quantum fluctuations** in driving macroscopic state switching within **biased Optical Parametric Oscillators**.
• Develops a framework for manipulating the **steady-state metapotential** via external bias fields to control stochastic transition timing.
• Bridges quantum optics and **non-von Neumann computing** by proposing mechanisms for **probabilistic quantum gates** and photonic AI.
key_results: Analytical derivation of average switching times as a function of vacuum noise.
Simulation-based validation of inter-state probability flow under bias injection.
Characterization of pump gain and nonlinearity effects on state stability.
Demonstration of bias-controlled energy landscape reshaping for deterministic noise-assisted switching.
Identification of OPO bistability as a primitive for high-speed stochastic machine learning.
