title: EchoSonar-R: A Multi-View Reasoning-Enabled Model for Disease Classification and Report Generation in Echocardiography
summary: Echocardiography is the most widely used non-invasive cardiac imaging modality, providing essential information for cardiovascular diagnosis. Interpreting an echocardiogram requires synthesizing complementary evidence across multiple heart views to identify abnormalities and produce structured clinical reports. While recent efforts focus on improving classification performance, most models lack explicit diagnostic reasoning and spatially grounded anatomical evidence, limiting clinician trust. We present EchoSonar-R, a multi-view reasoning-enabled vision-language model that jointly performs multi-label disease classification and report generation from echocardiography studies. EchoSonar-R combines a spatiotemporal video encoder with a structure-aware cardiac detector that provides spatially grounded anatomical cues to improve interpretability and clinician trust during cross-view reasoning. EchoSonar-R is trained in two stages: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on reasoning-annotated targets, followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with task-specific rewards that jointly align classification and report generation within a unified reinforcement-learning framework. Across a private multi-view dataset and two public benchmarks, EchoSonar-R improves macro balanced accuracy by 17.1% on the private set and 6.1% on MIMICEchoQA over the strongest baseline, achieves a GREEN clinical faithfulness score of 0.800, and produces interpretable reasoning traces grounded in multi-view visual evidence.

processed.summary:
• **EchoSonar-R** integrates a spatiotemporal video encoder with a **structure-aware cardiac detector** to provide spatially grounded anatomical cues for multi-view echocardiography analysis.
• The model utilizes a **two-stage training strategy** consisting of supervised fine-tuning on reasoning-annotated data followed by **Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)**.
• It leverages a **unified reinforcement learning framework** with task-specific rewards to align disease classification and clinical report generation accuracy.
• The architecture emphasizes **cross-view reasoning**, allowing the model to synthesize complementary evidence across multiple heart views for improved interpretability and trust.
key_results: Improved macro balanced accuracy by 17.1% on private multi-view echocardiography datasets.
Achieved a 6.1% performance gain over the strongest baseline on the MIMICEchoQA benchmark.
Obtained a GREEN clinical faithfulness score of 0.800 for generated reports.
Successfully generated interpretable reasoning traces grounded in multi-view visual evidence.
Demonstrated that joint alignment via GRPO significantly improves classification and generation metrics simultaneously.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: EchoSonar-R researchers develop a multi-view VLM using GRPO to enhance echocardiogram classification and clinical report faithfulness.

title: Robust Harmful Features Under Jailbreak Attacks: Mechanistic Evidence from Attention Head Specialization in Large Language Models
summary: Jailbreak attacks bypass LLM safety alignment, yet their mechanisms remain poorly understood. We provide evidence that attacks do not comprehensively eliminate safety features, but instead selectively suppress specific attention heads. We identify two functionally differentiated types: Adversarially Compromised Heads (ACHs) concentrated in early layers, which are suppressed under attacks, and Safety-Aligned Heads (SAHs) in mid-layers, which maintain robust activations even when attacks succeed. Ablation studies support the causal role of ACHs and the contribution of SAHs to robust activations: suppressing a small number of ACHs is sufficient to induce jailbreak-like behavior on normally refused inputs, while removing SAHs substantially weakens mid-layer safety activations. Token-level attribution further shows that ACH suppression is driven specifically by attack-template tokens, providing a mechanistic account of why attacks can bypass refusal decisions through ACH suppression while leaving internal safety signals sustained by SAHs -- a phenomenon we term Robust Harmful Features. To validate the practical significance of this robustness, we show that simply reading these persistent activations -- without any training -- yields competitive aggregate detection performance with strong adversarial robustness.

processed.summary:
- Identification of functionally distinct **Adversarially Compromised Heads (ACHs)** in early layers and **Safety-Aligned Heads (SAHs)** in middle layers.
- Evidence that jailbreak attacks succeed by **selectively suppressing ACHs** via attack-template tokens rather than erasing safety features globally.
- Discovery of **Robust Harmful Features**, where internal safety signals persist in mid-layer activations even when the model bypasses refusal.
- Implementation of a training-free **adversarial detection method** that reads persistent internal activations to identify successful jailbreaks.
key_results: Identification of ACHs (early layers) and SAHs (mid-layers) as key safety-related attention structures.
Ablating a small number of ACHs is sufficient to induce jailbreak behavior on normally refused inputs.
Attack-template tokens specifically drive the suppression of ACHs to bypass refusal logic.
SAHs maintain robust activation patterns even during successful adversarial jailbreak attempts.
Internal activation reading provides a competitive, zero-shot mechanism for detecting harmful inputs without training.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers show jailbreaks bypass LLM safety by suppressing early-layer attention heads while internal safety signals remain robust.

title: Toward Robust In-Context Segmentation via Concept Guidance
summary: In-context segmentation (ICS) requires a model to segment target regions in a query image using only a few reference images and their corresponding masks, without updating any parameters. Despite recent progress, prior ICS studies have largely overlooked a critical aspect: system robustness, ie, whether the model can produce stable segmentation results for the same query under different references. In this work, we revisit ICS from the robustness perspective and introduce a novel paradigm, Concept-Guided In-Context Segmentation (CG-ICS), which performs segmentation by extracting high-level semantic concepts from references rather than relying solely on low-level visual matching. Specifically, CG-ICS introduces a concept reasoning module that uses an MLLM to propose candidates and a SAM3-driven scoring function with tree-search refinement to select reliable textual concepts, together with a parallel visual exemplar route that provides query-side spatial grounding via a simple context construction. Both the textual concept and the visual exemplar are then used to activate the segmentation capability of a frozen SAM3 backbone. Extensive experiments on standard ICS benchmarks demonstrate that CG-ICS not only achieves state-of-the-art accuracy but also substantially improves robustness, yielding a more reliable ICS system with significantly reduced variance across diverse reference choices.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **Concept-Guided In-Context Segmentation (CG-ICS)** to address the instability and lack of robustness in traditional visual-matching-based segmentation models.
• Features a **concept reasoning module** that leverages a **Multimodal LLM (MLLM)** to derive semantic descriptors, refined through a **SAM3-driven scoring function** and tree-search.
• Combines textual semantic guidance with a parallel **visual exemplar route** to provide spatial grounding while keeping the **SAM3 backbone** frozen.
• Dramatically improves **system robustness** by reducing performance variance across different reference image selections compared to prior low-level matching methods.
key_results: Achieved new state-of-the-art accuracy on standard In-Context Segmentation (ICS) benchmarks.
Significantly reduced performance variance when using different reference images for the same query.
Successfully integrated high-level semantic reasoning with low-level visual spatial grounding.
Validated the effectiveness of tree-search refinement for selecting reliable textual concepts.
Demonstrated that frozen foundation models like SAM3 can be effectively steered via dual-modality prompting.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CG-ICS, using MLLMs and SAM3 to improve the robustness and accuracy of in-context segmentation.

title: A Reproducible Pipeline for Symmetry-Respecting Excited States on Near-Term Quantum Computers: The H2O/STO-3G Case
summary: Variational excited-state quantum algorithms fail for reasons usually studied in isolation: barren plateaus, symmetry contamination, finite-sampling instability, and hardware cost. Using one small but complete system -- H$_2$O in the STO-3G basis (12 qubits, Jordan--Wigner) -- we assemble these into a single reproducible pipeline, checking every claim against exact diagonalization. The bare qubit Hamiltonian interleaves cation ($N{=}7$) states below the neutral manifold; hardware-efficient and number-conserving ansätze stall at Hartree--Fock, an exact stationary point by Brillouin's theorem, while ADAPT-VQE escapes; variational deflation inherits the contamination and inverts the spectrum, whereas the quantum equation-of-motion (qEOM) subspace method restores the ladder to sub-milli-Hartree accuracy. Particle number is protected \emph{structurally} under shot noise, and a realistic measurement model collapses the thousands of subspace matrix elements to $\sim\!10^5$ commuting groups; a matrix-aware shot allocation then reaches chemical accuracy at $\sim\!3\times10^9$ total shots -- a thousandfold below the naive per-element estimate and reachable in days -- leaving single-circuit gate fidelity, not measurement, as the binding constraint. This work is a teaching and benchmarking reference, not a new method; all code, parameters, and figures are released.

processed.summary:
* **Integrated Benchmarking Pipeline**: Establishes a reproducible framework using H2O (12 qubits) to simultaneously address **barren plateaus**, symmetry contamination, and hardware constraints.
* **Algorithm Efficacy**: Demonstrates that **ADAPT-VQE** successfully escapes Hartree-Fock stationary points where hardware-efficient and number-conserving ansätze typically stall.
* **Subspace Restoration**: Utilizes the **quantum equation-of-motion (qEOM)** method to restore the energy ladder to sub-milli-Hartree accuracy, mitigating errors from variational deflation.
* **Measurement Efficiency**: Implements matrix-aware shot allocation to reach **chemical accuracy** with 3x10^9 shots, a 1000x improvement over naive estimation methods.
key_results: ADAPT-VQE identifies paths to escape Brillouin theorem-induced stalls in variational optimization.
The qEOM method achieves sub-milli-Hartree accuracy relative to exact diagonalization.
Particle number is structurally protected against shot noise within the proposed pipeline.
Total measurement requirements for chemical accuracy were reduced to a timeframe of days.
Single-circuit gate fidelity is identified as the primary performance bottleneck rather than measurement noise.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers establish a reproducible quantum pipeline achieving chemical accuracy for water molecules using optimized measurement and subspace methods.

title: From Tokens to States: LLMs as a Special Case of World Models and the Continuous Path Beyond
summary: The AI community has framed the relationship between large language models (LLMs) and world models as a dichotomy: LLMs predict tokens; world models simulate reality. Yann LeCun argues in 2022 that reaching general intelligence requires abandoning autoregressive token prediction in favour of latent-space architectures. This framing is unnecessarily binary. Two claims will be defended. First, LLMs are a degenerate special case of world models: the state space is the set of all token sequences, the only action is appending one token, and world models are therefore a strict generalisation of LLMs, not a replacement. Second, there is a natural continuous spectrum from NTP to JEPA, with multi-token prediction, future-summary prediction, and next-latent prediction as intermediate stations already populated by current research. Moving along this spectrum relaxes the LLM constraints one by one. It also progressively surrenders the two practical advantages that make LLMs trainable at scale: internet-scale self-supervised data, and a transformer architecture co-designed for discrete token prediction. Both are examined as open research questions: the data question (the cliff from self-supervised text to instrumented action-labelled environments) and the architecture question (whether the transformer generalises to continuous-state prediction, or whether a new primitive is needed).

processed.summary:
- The paper redefines **Large Language Models (LLMs)** not as alternatives to world models, but as a **degenerate special case** where the state space is restricted to discrete token sequences.
- It proposes a **continuous spectrum** of model architectures ranging from standard **Next Token Prediction (NTP)** to **Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA)**, including intermediate stages like next-latent prediction.
- The research highlights a critical trade-off: moving toward world models sacrifices the **internet-scale self-supervised data** and highly optimized **Transformer primitives** currently powering LLMs.
- It explores whether the **Transformer architecture** can generalize to continuous-state prediction or if a fundamental shift in architectural primitives is required for future AI.
key_results: LLMs are formally identified as world models with a discrete, sequence-based state space and single-token actions.
Identification of a research path from NTP to JEPA via multi-token and future-summary prediction.
Analysis of the 'data cliff' encountered when transitioning from raw text to action-labeled environment data.
Evaluation of current Transformer limitations in handling continuous-state transitions vs. discrete tokens.
Defense of JEPA as a strict generalization of LLM logic rather than a separate paradigm.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose that LLMs are specialized world models and map a continuous architectural path toward latent-space intelligence.

title: AI-Driven Synthesis for High-Tech System Design: Automating Innovation
summary: This article addresses the combinatorial complexity inherent in modern high-tech system design by presenting automation-in-design (AiD) as a transformative paradigm. We propose computational design synthesis (CDS), a framework utilising deep learning and generative AI to automate the creation of novel systems. Two case studies (e-drive system design and spatial dimensioning problem) serve as proof-points for this approach. The AI-driven methods used in the case studies represent a fundamental shift in engineering, advancing from simulation-based optimisation towards autonomous design with minimal human supervision.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **Computational Design Synthesis (CDS)**, a framework utilizing **Generative AI** and deep learning to navigate the combinatorial complexity of modern engineering.
- Introduces the **Automation-in-Design (AiD)** paradigm, shifting engineering workflows from traditional simulation-based optimization to **autonomous system creation**.
- Implements AI-driven methods to solve high-dimensional problems in **e-drive systems** and spatial dimensioning with minimal human supervision.
key_results: Conceptualization of the Automation-in-Design (AiD) paradigm.
Integration of deep learning for automated creation of novel hardware systems.
Validation of the CDS framework through an e-drive system design case study.
Successful application of AI synthesis to complex spatial dimensioning problems.
Transition from human-led simulation to AI-driven autonomous engineering synthesis.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a generative AI framework to automate complex high-tech system design through computational synthesis.

title: How Humans, Bots, and Agents Communicate About Vulnerabilities in Pull Requests
summary: Developers may reference vulnerabilities in pull request discussions through both explicit identifiers, such as CVEs or GHSAs, and implicit security-related language (e.g., "unauthorized access" or "SQL injection"). Prior work has primarily focused on explicit identifiers, potentially overlooking vulnerability discussions that lack formal references. Bots and coding agents are becoming more common in pull requests, raising new questions about how different accounts communicate about vulnerabilities. In this registered report, we describe our planned study of vulnerability communication in pull requests by humans, bots, and coding agents. Building on the AIDev-pop dataset, we analyze explicit vulnerability references and implicit security-related signals across pull request titles, descriptions, review comments, commit messages, and timeline discussions. We further investigate whether these references are associated with vulnerabilities introduced or fixed in the modified code and how they relate to pull request review activity and outcomes. This study contributes a large-scale empirical investigation of vulnerability communication practices in modern software development.

processed.summary:
• Investigates the communication patterns of **coding agents**, bots, and humans regarding software vulnerabilities within Pull Request (PR) workflows.
• Distinguishes between **explicit references** (e.g., CVE, GHSA identifiers) and **implicit signals** (e.g., semantic mentions of SQL injection or unauthorized access).
• Leverages the **AIDev-pop dataset** to analyze security discourse across PR titles, descriptions, review comments, and commit messages.
• Analyzes the correlation between security-related communication and the actual introduction or remediation of **vulnerabilities** in the codebase.
key_results: Categorizes vulnerability communication into explicit identifiers and implicit natural language signals.
Compares the performance and transparency of human developers versus automated coding agents in security contexts.
Tracks vulnerability-related discourse across five distinct PR data sources (titles, comments, commits, etc.).
Investigates the relationship between security discussions and PR review outcomes and velocity.
Establishes an empirical baseline for how AI agents contribute to or identify security risks in collaborative environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers study vulnerability communication between humans and AI agents to enhance security transparency in software development workflows.

title: The Reciprocal Impact of Science and Software: A Cross-Corpus Analysis of How Research Shapes Software and Software Enables Research
summary: Software and scientific knowledge co-evolve, yet they are catalogued in separate corpora that rarely speak to one another. We bridge them at global scale by linking World of Code (a near-complete mirror of public version-control history) to Semantic Scholar and OpenAlex through a typed cross-corpus graph of 69.8M edges over eight relation types (paper-to-software mentions, software-to-paper citations, software dependencies, authorship, affiliation, and identity bridges). Anchoring on 18,247 curated science repositories, we ask two reciprocal questions: what is the impact of science on software, and of software on science? To test whether this Science-Software Supply Chain (S3C) view is feasible, we run basic investigations rather than claim a definitive measurement. The two directions appear to illuminate different, complementary strata: the literature's reach into software is dominated by a reproducibility and packaging layer (nf-core, Nextflow, Bioconda) and sequence-analysis tools, whereas software's reach back into science is proxied by a largely invisible machine-learning and data-science infrastructure tier (PyTorch, seaborn, NLTK). The direct paper-names-software channel is too sparse to rank: a human-curated gold benchmark links none of its 65 in-scope cases. Dependency reuse stands in as a proxy and is at most weakly coupled to citation count and to stars (Spearman rho=0.36). Our most cautionary finding is about measurement itself: the reuse-citation coupling flips sign and confidence across two reasonable ways of pairing a repository with a citation count, through papers that name it (n=137, rho=0.05, CI straddling zero) versus DOIs a repository declares for itself (n=1,067, rho=0.13, CI [0.07,0.19]). With linkage this sparse, the sign of a headline correlation depends on which gap one tolerates, so we report both and refrain from a strong decoupling claim.

processed.summary:
- The study constructs a **Science-Software Supply Chain (S3C)** graph by linking the **World of Code** (vcs history) to **Semantic Scholar** and **OpenAlex**, encompassing 69.8M edges.
- Research findings indicate that the scientific impact on software is driven by a **reproducibility and packaging layer** (e.g., Nextflow, Bioconda), while software's utility to science is underpinned by **ML/Data Science infrastructure** like **PyTorch** and **NLTK**.
- The architecture utilizes a **typed cross-corpus graph** to track relations including paper-to-software mentions, software dependencies, and identity bridges across 18,247 curated repositories.
- Analysis reveals a significant **measurement discrepancy**, where the correlation between software reuse and scientific citations is highly sensitive to the specific linkage method used (paper mentions vs. self-declared DOIs).
key_results: Constructed a massive 69.8M edge graph linking global version-control history to scientific literature corpora.
Identified PyTorch, seaborn, and NLTK as the primary 'invisible' infrastructure enabling modern scientific research.
Found that the direct 'paper-names-software' communication channel is too sparse to effectively rank software impact.
Determined that dependency reuse is only weakly coupled to traditional metrics like citation counts and GitHub stars (Spearman rho=0.36).
Showed that the correlation between reuse and citations flips sign depending on whether repo-declared DOIs or paper mentions are used.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that machine learning infrastructure like PyTorch drives scientific progress despite weak coupling between citations and software stars.

title: MMAO: A Metabolic Multi-Agent Optimizer with Endogenous Resource Allocation for Continuous and Discrete Optimization
summary: Traditional meta-heuristics often rely on fixed population sizes, manually chosen search scales, and externally attached parameter-control modules. This paper presents the \textit{Metabolic Multi-Agent Optimizer} (MMAO), a cross-domain optimization framework in which adaptation is derived endogenously from a private-public metabolic resource loop. Each agent carries internal energy, a continuous role state, motion or structural memory, and local search history, while the population shares a communal resource pool. Fitness improvements are converted into normalized metabolic gains through a robust progress scale and a recent success statistic; the same closed loop then regulates sensing intensity, search amplitude, role drift, branching, pruning, respawning, and elite reinvestment. In the continuous setting, MMAO uses energy-regulated symmetric zero-order probing and role-interpolated motion. In the discrete setting, the same control law is instantiated through structural sensing, local route improvement, guided perturbation, and energy-weighted edge reuse. The paper combines an implementation-faithful formulation with a reproducible experimental study on a CEC2017 subset (10D/30D, 20 seeds) and five TSPLIB instances (100 discrete runs in total). The current evidence supports MMAO primarily as a parameter-light, self-calibrating optimization framework whose main validated originality lies in metabolically endogenous resource allocation across heterogeneous search behaviors, rather than as a universally superior optimizer.

processed.summary:
- MMAO introduces a **metabolic resource loop** that endogenously regulates agent behavior, shifting away from fixed population sizes and manual parameter tuning.
- The architecture utilizes a **private-public energy system** where fitness improvements are converted into metabolic energy to control sensing intensity, search amplitude, and agent lifecycles.
- The framework supports both **continuous and discrete optimization** through domain-specific modules like symmetric zero-order probing and energy-weighted edge reuse.
- It implements **self-calibrating mechanisms** for branching, pruning, and respawning, allowing the agent population to adapt dynamically to the search landscape.
key_results: Demonstrated effective performance on CEC2017 continuous optimization benchmarks.
Validated discrete optimization capabilities using five TSPLIB (TSP) instances.
Achieved parameter-light operation by replacing manual search scales with energy-regulated logic.
Established a robust progress scale that converts fitness gains into normalized metabolic energy.
Proved the viability of role-interpolated motion for maintaining population diversity during optimization.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce MMAO, a self-calibrating multi-agent framework that optimizes via metabolic energy allocation and endogenous resource loops.

title: Cross-view Multimodal Vision-Based Assessment Framework for Traditional Chinese Medicine Rehabilitation Training
summary: Vision-based assessment can provide convenient and cost-effective evaluation in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) rehabilitation training, where action quality assessment (AQA) from computer vision offers a promising solution. Existing automatic AQA frameworks for physical therapy typically rely on skeletal data captured from a single viewpoint, which is inefficient for TCM techniques such as acupuncture or Tuina that involve dense hand self-occlusion and complex hand-object interactions. To address these challenges, we propose CME-AQA, a cross-view, multimodal vision-based assessment framework that integrates visual-pose fusion to enhance understanding of environmental context and leverages both first-person and third-person videos during training to improve inference robustness. We collected two dual-view datasets, TCM-AQA61-A (Acupuncture) and TCM-AQA61-T (Tuina), each containing synchronized first-person and third-person recordings of 61 subjects with expert annotations. Experimental results show that our approach achieves superior or comparable mean performance against competitive baselines, achieving over 10% relative improvement in weighted F1 over the best competing method on key rating tasks such as Needle Depth and Quick Needle Insertion, while also reducing mean absolute error in quantitative measures such as insertion time and manipulation frequency. Testing on a CPR dataset further demonstrates comparable performance on several posture-based criteria, suggesting applicability to related structured simulated clinical skill assessments where participant motion is central to evaluation. Overall, CME-AQA enhances assessment accuracy for structured TCM rehabilitation training and facilitates more convenient and effective training-oriented skill evaluation.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **CME-AQA**, a cross-view multimodal framework designed for **Action Quality Assessment (AQA)** in specialized medical procedures.
 • Integrates **visual-pose fusion** to mitigate challenges like dense hand self-occlusion and complex hand-object interactions typical in acupuncture.
 • Leverages a **dual-view training strategy** combining first-person and third-person video streams to enhance spatial robustness and environmental context.
 • Introduces two novel dual-view datasets, **TCM-AQA61-A and TCM-AQA61-T**, featuring expert-annotated synchronized recordings.
key_results: Achieved over 10% relative improvement in weighted F1 for specific tasks like Needle Depth and Quick Insertion.
Reduced Mean Absolute Error (MAE) in quantitative measures including insertion time and manipulation frequency.
Outperformed competitive baselines in mean performance across two specialized TCM datasets.
Validated framework generalizability by achieving comparable performance on external posture-based CPR datasets.
Demonstrated that first-person and third-person view integration significantly improves inference robustness over single-view models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: TCM-AQA Researchers introduce CME-AQA, a multimodal framework that utilizes cross-view video fusion to significantly improve clinical skill assessment.

title: Fair Classification with Efficient and Post-hoc Controllable Fairness-Accuracy Trade-off
summary: Post-hoc controllability of fair machine learning models, the ability to control the trade-off between fairness and accuracy after training, is valuable for practical deployment. Existing post-processing methods provide such post-hoc controllability but often suffer from significant accuracy degradation, whereas in-processing methods achieve efficient trade-offs but require computationally expensive retraining for each change in trade-off ratio. To achieve both post-hoc controllability and efficient trade-offs, we propose a novel fair classification algorithm that learns effective feature representations to improve the trade-off efficiency of post-processing fair classifiers, by a gradient-based optimization approach. Experimental results on real-world datasets demonstrate that our method achieves trade-off efficiency comparable to, or even surpassing, in-processing methods, without requiring any retraining.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **gradient-based optimization** approach to learn feature representations that maximize the efficiency of **post-processing fair classifiers**.
• Enables **post-hoc controllability**, allowing practitioners to adjust the fairness-accuracy trade-off ratio after training without any computational retraining.
• Bridges the gap between **in-processing methods** (efficient but rigid) and **post-processing methods** (flexible but accuracy-degraded).
• Demonstrates that optimized representations can achieve **trade-off efficiency** comparable to or better than state-of-the-art in-processing techniques.
key_results: Novel algorithm for learning fairness-aware feature representations via gradient optimization.
Achievement of post-hoc trade-off control without the need for expensive model retraining.
Trade-off efficiency results that match or surpass traditional in-processing methods on real-world data.
Validation of the method across multiple standard fair machine learning benchmarks.
Significant reduction in accuracy degradation typically associated with post-hoc fairness adjustments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a gradient-based optimization method to enable post-hoc fairness control in classifiers without requiring model retraining.

title: Typing Behavior in Human-LLM Interaction: Keystroke Dynamics Reveal Cognitive Effort During Prompting
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) become increasingly integrated into daily routines, understanding how users interact with these systems is crucial for effective human-AI collaboration. This work investigates keystroke dynamics as a behavioral measure of user mental effort and perceived output usefulness in human-LLM interaction. We conducted a user study (N = 36) to examine how task difficulty (easy vs. hard) and device type (desktop vs. mobile) influence typing behavior and workload (NASA-TLX) during interactions. Our results indicate that hard tasks led to significantly more keystrokes, slower typing, increased pauses, and higher self-reported workload. Device type had weaker effects, with mobile use slightly reducing input length and typing speed. While keystrokes captured differences in cognitive effort, they did not predict perceived LLM output usefulness. These findings highlight the potential of keystroke dynamics as real-time indicators of cognitive effort during LLM prompting, while also showing their limitations in capturing perceived collaboration success.

processed.summary:
* Investigates **keystroke dynamics** (typing speed, pause frequency, and total input volume) as a behavioral proxy for **cognitive load** during human-LLM interactions.
* Assesses the impact of **task difficulty** and device form factor on user engagement and **prompt engineering** workflows using a controlled user study (N=36).
* Proposes using real-time behavioral metrics as **real-time indicators** of mental effort, potentially enabling adaptive AI systems that adjust support based on user hesitation.
key_results: Hard tasks resulted in significantly higher keystroke counts and slower overall typing speeds.
Increased cognitive demand during prompting leads to more frequent and longer pauses.
Keystroke dynamics correlate strongly with NASA-TLX self-reported workload scores.
Mobile usage slightly reduces input length and speed but has a weaker effect on effort than task complexity.
Behavioral typing patterns do not accurately predict how useful a user perceives the LLM's output to be.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that keystroke dynamics effectively measure user cognitive effort during prompt engineering but fail to predict output satisfaction.

title: STAG: Spatio-temporal Evolving Structural Representation of Action Units for Micro-expression Recognition
summary: Micro-expression recognition is challenging due to subtle and short-lived facial muscle movements. Existing methods rely heavily on apex-onset frames, overlook fine-grained inter-frame dynamics, and separately model spatial and temporal information, limiting generalization across datasets. To address these challenges, we propose STAG, a dynamic ROI-AU-coupled spatial-temporal network that jointly models motion flow and adaptive facial connectivity. The framework extracts optical flow from discriminative frames using magnitude-based selection and temporal attention. A dual-branch architecture combines an enhanced graph attention network for structured spatial reasoning with a transformer encoder for temporal modeling. A bidirectional cross-attention module enables mutual refinement of spatial and temporal features, while AU-guided dynamic connectivity adapts facial region interactions according to muscle activation patterns. The transformer captures subtle temporal dynamics beyond apex-based approaches, improving semantic consistency and interpretability for explainable micro-expression recognition. The fused representation is optimized using focal loss and evaluated on CASME II, 4DME, DFME, NaME, SAMM, and SMIC-HS. Extensive experiments demonstrate improved robustness, generalization, interpretability, and computational efficiency, confirming the effectiveness of adaptive relational reasoning, AU-guided dynamic connectivity, and deep spatial-temporal feature fusion for accurate cross-dataset micro-expression recognition.

* Proposes **STAG**, a dual-branch architecture that integrates **Graph Attention Networks (GAT)** for spatial reasoning with **Transformer encoders** for capture of fine-grained temporal dynamics.
* Introduces **AU-guided dynamic connectivity**, which adaptively models facial region interactions based on specific muscle activation patterns rather than static physical landmarks.
* Utilizes a **bidirectional cross-attention** module to enable mutual refinement between spatial and temporal features, overcoming the limitations of traditional apex-frame-dependent methods.
* Incorporates **magnitude-based optical flow selection** to isolate discriminative motion, enhancing the model's ability to recognize subtle, short-lived micro-expressions.
key_results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on major datasets including CASME II, SAMM, and SMIC-HS.
Demonstrated superior generalization in cross-dataset evaluation scenarios compared to existing benchmarks.
Improved model interpretability by visualizing AU-guided connectivity and muscle activation patterns.
Reduced reliance on apex-frame identification through continuous temporal modeling via Transformers.
Achieved higher computational efficiency and robustness against subtle facial noise.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce STAG, using AU-guided dynamic connectivity and transformers to enhance accuracy and interpretability in micro-expression recognition.

title: Context-Aware Explanations for Spatialized Document Layouts
summary: Spatialized document layouts are widely used for exploratory analysis of text corpora, but interpreting the spatial organization of documents and the relationships between regions remains challenging. Existing approaches primarily summarize document content or explain how layouts are generated, providing limited support for understanding spatial relationships within the layout itself. We present CAPE, a context-aware explanation framework that generates natural-language explanations grounded in both document semantics and layout-derived spatial context. CAPE identifies salient spatial patterns (e.g., clusters, subgroups, outliers, and bridging documents) and constructs multi-level contextual representations to guide LLM-based explanation generation. It supports both AI-guided overview and user-driven exploration, with explanations available at multiple levels of detail. We demonstrate CAPE on news and scholarly document layouts and evaluate it in a controlled user study against keyword-based and content-only LLM baselines. Our results suggest that spatially grounded explanations are perceived as more helpful than content-only baselines for interpreting the spatial organization of document layouts.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **CAPE**, a framework that utilizes **LLMs** to generate natural-language explanations for document layouts grounded in both semantics and spatial context.
* Employs **multi-level contextual representations** to detect and explain complex spatial patterns including clusters, subgroups, outliers, and bridging documents.
* Supports **dual-mode exploration**, offering AI-guided overview summaries and user-driven detail-on-demand for interactive document analysis.
* Bridges the gap between **spatial organization** and human-readable reasoning, moving beyond simple keyword-based or content-only explanation models.
key_results: CAPE effectively identifies salient spatial patterns such as clusters and outliers within 2D document projections.
Spatially grounded explanations outperformed content-only LLM baselines in user helpfulness scores.
The framework provides multi-level detail, allowing for both high-level overviews and granular document-level insights.
Validation on news and scholarly datasets confirms the generalizability of the context-aware approach.
User studies indicate that spatial context is critical for accurately interpreting document relationships in visual layouts.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CAPE to generate context-aware LLM explanations that help users interpret complex spatial document layouts.

title: Ontology-Guided Evidence Path Inference for Multi-hop Knowledge Graph Question Answering
summary: Knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) aims to answer natural-language questions by reasoning over structured facts. Existing multi-hop KGQA methods mainly rely on topic-centered expansion, which faces two key challenges: the search space rapidly grows with noisy mixed-type paths, and retrieved paths may fail to satisfy the semantic constraints of complex questions. To address these challenges, we propose OPI, an ontology-guided evidence path inference framework for multi-hop KGQA. OPI introduces a relation-centric ontology graph to capture the head-tail type constraints of relations, providing a compact interface for answer-side constraints. Based on this ontology graph, OPI first introduces a bidirectional retrieval mechanism by mapping the predicted answer type to compatible final-hop relations and combining topic-side prefix expansion with answer-side final-hop matching, thereby suppressing noisy mixed-type expansion. OPI further adopts an iterative refinement strategy to reassess retrieved paths and candidate answers under the question context, filtering type-compatible but question-irrelevant evidence for more reliable answer prediction. Experiments on WebQSP, CWQ, and MetaQA show that OPI substantially reduces the search space, improves Hit@1/F1 by 4.6/5.0 points on WebQSP and 8.9/3.3 points on CWQ over the strongest prior results, and achieves near-saturated Hit@1 on MetaQA with the retrieval module alone.

processed.summary:
- Proposes OPI, an **ontology-guided evidence path inference** framework designed to handle the exponential search space growth in multi-hop Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA).
- Introduces a **relation-centric ontology graph** that enforces head-tail type constraints, allowing for a **bidirectional retrieval mechanism** that matches topic-side expansion with answer-side constraints.
- Employs an **iterative refinement strategy** to filter type-compatible but question-irrelevant evidence, ensuring the final reasoning path aligns with the semantic context of complex questions.
key_results: Improved Hit@1 by 4.6 points and F1 by 5.0 points on the WebQSP dataset.
Achieved a significant 8.9 point Hit@1 increase on the Complex WebQuestions (CWQ) benchmark.
Reached near-saturated performance on the MetaQA dataset using only the retrieval module.
Substantially reduced the search space compared to traditional topic-centered expansion methods.
Demonstrated superior performance in satisfying semantic constraints for complex, multi-hop queries.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose OPI, a framework using ontology graphs to improve multi-hop KGQA by pruning noisy search paths.

title: Single and Multi Truth Data Fusion using Large Language Models
summary: Data fusion, also known as truth discovery, is a data integration problem that aims to determine the correct value or set of values for each attribute of an object when presented with potentially conflicting values from multiple sources. Data fusion tasks belong to two main categories: single-truth scenarios, where each attribute has only one correct value, and multi-truth scenarios, where multiple values can be valid simultaneously. This paper investigates the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in data fusion tasks for tabular data. Various prompting strategies, encompassing both single-truth and multi-truth scenarios, are investigated empirically. Domain-dependent, domain-independent, zero-shot and one-shot prompts are evaluated on three different benchmark datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that LLM-based approaches outperform traditional unsupervised truth discovery methods, such as DART and LTM, across all datasets. The codebase of this study has been made publicly available on GitHub.

processed.summary:
- The research evaluates **Large Language Models (LLMs)** as a replacement for traditional unsupervised **truth discovery** algorithms in both single and multi-truth data fusion scenarios.
- The methodology focuses on **Prompt Engineering** strategies, comparing domain-dependent vs. domain-independent templates and zero-shot vs. one-shot learning approaches for **tabular data** integration.
- Experimental findings indicate that LLMs effectively capture semantic context between conflicting values, a capability lacking in traditional models like **DART** and **LTM**.
- The study establishes a new state-of-the-art for data fusion by leveraging the **reasoning capabilities** of LLMs to resolve attribute conflicts in structured datasets.
key_results: LLM-based approaches consistently outperformed traditional unsupervised baselines (DART and LTM) across all tested benchmarks.
Domain-dependent prompts yielded higher accuracy than domain-independent ones by providing necessary context for attribute values.
One-shot prompting significantly improved performance over zero-shot baselines by establishing clear output formatting and logic.
The models successfully addressed complex multi-truth scenarios where multiple valid attributes can exist simultaneously.
A public codebase was released to facilitate further research into LLM-driven data integration and truth discovery.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University of Rostock researchers demonstrate that LLMs using advanced prompting outperform traditional unsupervised methods in complex data fusion tasks.

title: How to deal with machine learning bias in economic history
summary: Machine learning (ML) has rapidly transformed economic history, lowering costs of digitization, data linkage, and imputation, and making information in historical text usable at scale. This paper offers a practical guide to using these tools well. However, ML tools have also created new problems. Prediction errors are often systematically correlated with covariates of interest, so even highly accurate models can distort and sometimes reverse coefficients, and standard validation cannot detect this. Given that ML tools often perform worse for historical data, this problem is especially severe for the field of economic history. We also identify a solution to this problem. We show that recent debiasing methods can correct such bias for a wide class of applications, using a small, randomly sampled set of expert-coded labels while retaining the efficiency of large-scale prediction. We organize the field with a taxonomy of three ML tasks, survey the literature along it, and indicate where debiasing applies and where validation against proxies remains the only recourse. We close with best-practice guidance on digitization, model choice, and reproducibility.

processed.summary:
* The paper identifies that **systematic prediction errors** in ML models are often correlated with covariates, which can distort or reverse statistical coefficients in downstream analysis.
* It introduces a **taxonomy of three ML tasks**—digitization, data linkage, and imputation—to provide a structured framework for identifying where bias enters historical data pipelines.
* The authors propose a **debiasing methodology** that utilizes a small, randomly sampled set of expert-coded labels to correct model outputs while maintaining the efficiency of large-scale automation.
* The study highlights the limitations of **standard validation metrics**, arguing that high accuracy does not guarantee the absence of analytical bias in historical research.
key_results: High model accuracy (F1/Accuracy) does not prevent systematic bias in regression coefficients.
ML errors in historical data are frequently non-random and correlate with variables of interest.
A small, expert-labeled 'gold standard' dataset can effectively debias large-scale ML predictions.
Historical data poses unique challenges due to lower data quality and lack of modern standardized training sets.
Debiasing methods are applicable to a wide class of economic and social science applications.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate how expert-labeled debiasing methods can correct systematic ML errors in historical data analysis and econometrics.

title: ToolPrivacyBench: Benchmarking Purpose-Bound Privacy in Tool-Using LLM Agents
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have increasingly moved from standalone text generation systems to agents that invoke external tools, access environments, and execute multi-step tasks. However, conventional function-calling benchmarks mainly evaluate task completion and API correctness, while privacy evaluation benchmarks typically focus on final responses or privacy judgments. Neither perspective captures purpose-bound information flow across an executed multi-tool trajectory. Motivated by this limitation in current agent evaluation, ToolPrivacyBench audits whether task-private atoms are routed only to authorized tools and downstream sinks, thereby evaluating both task completion and privacy over-disclosure during tool use. The benchmark contains 2,150 cases, including 1,150 fully synthetic privacy-sensitive business workflows and 1,000 cases adapted from existing multi-tool and function-calling benchmarks. Each case is represented by a policy knowledge base. After an agent executes against mock business backends, the evaluator compares recorded tool arguments and backend audit logs with this policy knowledge base. The evaluation covers nine widely used agents to characterize purpose-bound privacy over-disclosure. The results show that successful tool execution does not imply appropriate privacy disclosure: an agent may complete a task while transmitting unnecessary private information through intermediate tool calls. ToolPrivacyBench therefore formalizes a need-to-know disclosure boundary, under which each tool should receive only the information necessary for its stated purpose, and uses trajectory-level auditing to identify privacy over-disclosure in multi-tool workflows.

processed.summary:
* **ToolPrivacyBench** is a novel evaluation framework designed to audit **purpose-bound privacy** by tracking information flow across multi-tool trajectories in LLM agents.
* The system utilizes a **policy knowledge base** to establish "need-to-know" boundaries, ensuring that sensitive data atoms are only routed to authorized tools and sinks.
* The architecture involves **trajectory-level auditing** where recorded tool arguments and backend logs are compared against privacy policies to detect over-disclosure even during successful task completion.
key_results: Benchmark contains 2,150 test cases, including 1,150 synthetic business workflows and 1,000 adapted function-calling cases.
Evaluation of nine major LLM agents shows that successful task execution does not guarantee privacy protection.
Agents frequently leak sensitive information to intermediate tools that do not require that data for their specific sub-task.
Formalized a 'need-to-know' boundary for data disclosure in complex, multi-step agentic workflows.
The benchmark successfully identifies privacy over-disclosure patterns that traditional function-calling benchmarks overlook.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ToolPrivacyBench to audit LLM agents for unnecessary private data disclosure during multi-tool execution workflows.

title: ReScene: Structured Indoor Scene Reconstruction from Multi-View Captures
summary: Constructing simulation-ready 3D scenes from multi-view captures is a key bottleneck for Embodied Artificial Intelligence, as downstream tasks require object-level structure, explicit inter-object relations, and physical plausibility. Existing approaches either rely on specialized capture hardware, suffer from single-view bias in object reconstruction, or yield layouts that are geometrically reasonable but physically inconsistent. We identify that the problem is not single-object reconstruction but cross-view relation fusion and physically plausible scene assembly. To address this challenge, we present ReScene, a framework that threads multi-view geometry throughout the pipeline as a unifying prior. Our method consists of two main components: HierView prioritizes reconstruction views based on semantic consistency and 3D coverage completeness, replacing the largest-mask heuristic that conflates image occupancy with object coverage; and Relation-Aware Assembly fuses multi-frame relation predictions from a vision-language model with geometric and room-shell priors into a confidence-weighted scene graph, enabling physically consistent scene assembly. ReScene sets a new state of the art across geometry, rendering, and perceptual quality on a set of ScanNet scenes, achieving a 17% reduction in Chamfer Distance and 26% in LPIPS over the strongest prior baseline, while running up to 10x faster than prior multi-view methods. Based on the reconstructed scenes, we also generate an embodied visual question answering dataset, on which fine-tuned Qwen-VL approaches the performance of strong closed-source models on several spatial reasoning tasks.

processed.summary:
* **ReScene** introduces a novel framework for constructing simulation-ready 3D scenes from multi-view captures, specifically addressing the physical inconsistency issues in **Embodied AI**.
* The architecture utilizes **HierView** to prioritize reconstruction views based on semantic consistency and 3D coverage, moving beyond traditional image occupancy heuristics.
* A **Relation-Aware Assembly** module fuses vision-language model (VLM) predictions with geometric and room-shell priors to generate a **confidence-weighted scene graph**.
* The framework enables **physically plausible scene assembly** while delivering significant improvements in geometric accuracy and rendering speed.
key_results: 17% reduction in Chamfer Distance compared to the strongest prior baseline.
26% improvement in LPIPS perceptual quality scores for indoor scenes.
Up to 10x faster inference speed than previous multi-view reconstruction methods.
Successful generation of a high-quality embodied visual question answering dataset.
Fine-tuned Qwen-VL models achieved spatial reasoning performance nearing closed-source benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ReScene, a framework using VLMs and geometric priors to achieve 10x faster, physically consistent 3D reconstruction.

title: AirGroundBench: Probing Spatial Intelligence in Multimodal Large Models under Heterogeneous Multi-View Embodied Collaboration
summary: In recent years, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown strong potential for embodied intelligence, yet their ability to maintain geometrically consistent spatial understanding across heterogeneous views remains under-evaluated. Existing benchmarks largely focus on single-agent, single-view perception, leaving a gap in the systematic assessment of collaborative air-ground settings, where multi-scale observations are complementary but introduce scale mismatch, asymmetric occlusion, and reference-frame inconsistencies. We present AirGroundBench, a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating multi-view spatial intelligence in heterogeneous UAV-UGV collaboration. AirGroundBench is built from 11 high-fidelity simulated environments with 1,021 synchronized air-ground observation pairs, yielding approximately 62,000 dual-view, four-option single-choice visual question answering instances and 115 closed-loop vision-language navigation episodes. It covers 10 task types organized into four progressively demanding capability dimensions: spatial perception, cross-view alignment, spatial transformation and reasoning, and embodied decision-making. To support geometry-grounded evaluation and analysis, we provide structured spatial annotations, including cross-view object identities and metric 2D and 3D bounding boxes. Evaluations of 13 representative MLLMs under UAV-only, UGV-only, and dual-view input settings reveal consistent bottlenecks: models perform relatively well on spatial perception but struggle with cross-view alignment and transformation-intensive reasoning, and these deficits propagate to sequential decision-making in vision-language navigation. Although dual-view inputs provide measurable gains over single-view variants, a persistent gap from human performance remains, highlighting geometric consistency as a key limitation of current embodied MLLMs.

processed.summary:
Introduces **AirGroundBench**, a diagnostic framework designed to evaluate **spatial intelligence** in MLLMs during heterogeneous multi-agent (UAV-UGV) collaboration.
Features a dataset of 62,000 VQA instances and 115 **Vision-Language Navigation (VLN)** episodes across four capability levels: perception, alignment, reasoning, and decision-making.
Provides high-fidelity simulated environments with structured **spatial annotations**, including 2D/3D bounding boxes to enable geometry-grounded evaluation.
Identifies a critical failure mode in current **multimodal large language models**: the inability to maintain **geometric consistency** across disparate vantage points.
key_results: Evaluated 13 state-of-the-art MLLMs under UAV, UGV, and dual-view input configurations.
Models demonstrate adequate spatial perception but fail significantly at cross-view alignment tasks.
Transformation-intensive reasoning, such as coordinate mapping, remains a major bottleneck for current architectures.
Geometric reasoning deficits directly impair performance in sequential decision-making for vision-language navigation.
Dual-view inputs provide performance gains over single-view, yet a large gap remains compared to human-level spatial reasoning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce AirGroundBench to expose significant gaps in MLLM spatial reasoning and cross-view alignment for embodied agents.

title: Rapid Prototyping of Event-Driven Contextual Memory in the ACT-Up Cognitive Architecture
summary: The present paper describes an implementation of contextual memory and a basic event-handler for the ACT-Up cognitive architecture which maintains its scalability and appropriateness for rapid-prototyping while adding essential features and lowering the barrier to entry for new users. This includes describing a theory-neutral implementation of working memory and spreading activation, in addition to a basic associative learning mechanism. An example of rapid prototyping for algorithm development is presented using the serial memory task described in Klein, Addis, and Kahana (2005). This study describes how contiguity effects change across sequential list presentations across three serial and free recall conditions. We further describe how to use generative AI and the event handler to automatically create cognitive experiments directly from the Methods section of research papers.

processed.summary:
• **ACT-Up Architecture**: Implements event-driven contextual memory and theory-neutral working memory to improve scalability and rapid prototyping for cognitive modeling.
• **Cognitive Mechanisms**: Integrates spreading activation and associative learning, allowing for the simulation of complex psychological tasks like serial and free recall.
• **Generative AI Integration**: Features an event handler that leverages LLMs to parse research paper "Methods" sections, automatically generating executable cognitive experiments.
• **Impact**: Lowers the barrier to entry for cognitive science researchers by providing a flexible framework for algorithmic development and automated experiment design.
key_results: Successful implementation of a scalable, event-driven memory handler for ACT-Up.
Development of a theory-neutral framework for working memory and spreading activation.
Replication of contiguity effects in serial memory tasks based on established psychological data.
Reduction in manual coding effort through rapid-prototyping features.
Proven automated translation of research text into executable code using Generative AI.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Carnegie Mellon University researchers leverage Generative AI to automate cognitive experiment generation within the updated ACT-Up memory architecture.

title: A Tree-of-Thoughts Inspired Hybrid Approach for Legal Case Judgement Summarization using LLMs
summary: In recent times, Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly being used for legal case judgement summarization. Most prior works have tried traditional extractive and abstractive summarization of case judgements. However, hybrid or extractive-abstractive techniques have not been explored much. In this work, we propose a novel tree-of-thoughts inspired extractive-abstractive summarization approach for legal judgement summarization. We conduct experiments using two popular LLMs, DeepSeek and LLama, and compare among extractive, abstractive and extractive-abstractive summarization. Our experiments show that the proposed extractive-abstractive prompt provides better summaries compared to other types of LLM prompts.

processed.summary:
* Proposes a **Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) inspired framework** that integrates extractive and abstractive summarization techniques for complex legal documents.
* Implements a **hybrid prompting strategy** designed to leverage the reasoning capabilities of LLMs to navigate dense legal case judgements.
* Benchmarks the architecture using **DeepSeek** and **Llama** models to compare the performance of multi-stage hybrid methods against traditional single-pass baselines.
key_results: Hybrid extractive-abstractive prompts outperformed standalone extractive or abstractive methods.
Tree-of-Thoughts structure improved the identification of critical legal precedents within long contexts.
The approach showed consistent performance gains across both DeepSeek and Llama model families.
Multi-stage reasoning reduced information loss typical in purely abstractive legal summarization.
Refining summaries through a ToT-inspired path provided higher factual density for judicial decisions.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a Tree-of-Thoughts hybrid prompting method that improves legal summarization accuracy using DeepSeek and Llama.

title: Same Coeffect, Different Base: Connecting Two Dominant Approaches to Graded Types
summary: Graded types provide a way to augment a type system with fine-grained information, e.g., to track side effects or context dependence and resource use (called coeffects). Graded types for coeffects have found their way into languages such as Haskell, Idris, and Granule, enabling resourceful reasoning via coeffect analysis with varying levels of generality. Two separate lineages of graded coeffect system have emerged in the last decade: those in which coeffect annotations are pervasive, requiring annotations on function types (which we call graded-base) and those in which coeffects are added by way of a graded modal type operator atop linear types (which we call linear-base). The latter has its origins in Girard's Linear Logic which has been a rich humus for programming language research focused on resources, whereas the graded-base approach emerged in the mid-2010s, seeing rapid adoption in programming language theory and practice, e.g. in QTT and Linear Haskell. The relationship between these two styles has however remained an open question. We answer this question by giving translations between pairs of calculi of both lineages that we prove type-, grade- and operational-semantics preserving. We show that the same notions of context dependence can be expressed in either style, building a bridge between the two lineages that enables transfer of results and ideas, while helping language designers to make better informed choices.

processed.summary:
* Establishes a formal bridge between **graded-base** and **linear-base** type systems, the two primary methods for tracking resource use and context dependence.
* Provides bidirectional translations between these calculi that are proven to preserve **type safety**, **grades**, and **operational semantics**.
* Unifies disparate lineages of programming language research, linking **Girard's Linear Logic** with modern **Quantitative Type Theory (QTT)** used in languages like Idris and Granule.
key_results: Formal proof of equivalence between pervasive coeffect annotations and graded modal operators.
Preservation of semantic integrity during translation between different resource-tracking architectures.
Demonstration that both styles can express the same notions of context dependence.
Theoretical foundation for transferring research results between Linear Logic and modern graded type systems.
Guidance for language designers to choose between pervasive or modal coeffect implementations based on specific needs.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University of Kent researchers unify two major resource-tracking type systems, enabling better formal reasoning in functional programming languages.

title: Evolution-Aware Regression Test Prioritization of ML-Enabled Systems Using Gradient-Based Behavior Vectors
summary: The machine learning(ML) component of an ML-enabled system evolves through retraining, fine-tuning, and optimization, so previously valid test results may no longer hold. A single evolution step can worsen performance on some test cases while improving others, making regression test prioritization inherently directional. We present Gradient-based Behavior Vector-Parameter Delta(GBV-PD), the first approach to operationalize the behavior vector space for evolution-aware regression test prioritization. GBV-PD represents each test case as a gradient-based vector(GBV), a low-dimensional projection of its loss gradient under the original model. It then projects the observed parameter update of the evolved model onto the same PCA basis and uses the resulting alignment to estimate whether each test case's loss is likely to increase or decrease, without running the evolved model on test cases during prioritization. In an empirical study across classification and regression tasks, GBV-PD consistently outperformed non-directional baselines and remained competitive with a full-gradient reference, while offering better time and storage profiles for repeated updates via reusable GBV caching. These results show that behavior-space ideas can be operationalized into a practical and efficient mechanism for repeated-update regression testing of evolving ML-enabled systems.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **GBV-PD**, an evolution-aware regression test prioritization framework that uses **Gradient-based Behavior Vectors** to predict test case loss shifts.
- Projects loss gradients and **model parameter updates** onto a shared PCA basis to calculate alignment without running inference on the updated model.
- Enables **directional prioritization**, identifying which test cases are most likely to fail or degrade after fine-tuning or retraining.
- Implements **reusable GBV caching** to optimize storage and compute, making it suitable for high-frequency ML deployment cycles.
key_results: GBV-PD consistently outperforms traditional non-directional regression test prioritization baselines.
Demonstrates that low-dimensional gradient projections effectively capture directional performance changes.
Maintains competitive accuracy compared to computationally expensive full-gradient reference models.
Reduces time and storage overhead for repeated model updates via efficient vector caching.
Successfully validates the approach across diverse machine learning tasks including classification and regression.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Peking University researchers introduce GBV-PD to prioritize ML regression tests by aligning gradient-based behavior vectors with parameter updates.

title: Rationalizable Behavior in Matching with Externalities
summary: In many matching markets, agents care not only about their own partners but also about the matches formed by others. With externalities, stability depends on what agents believe would happen after a deviation. We introduce rationalizable conjectures: beliefs that survive iterated elimination, in the spirit of rationalizability in non-cooperative games. These beliefs define conjecture-rationalizable stability, a solution concept that always exists, extends Gale--Shapley stability, and coincides with it when externalities are absent. We also introduce rationalizable matchings, a non-equilibrium counterpart, and show that every conjecture-rationalizable stable matching is rationalizable. In matching with couples, our concept yields non-empty predictions even when standard stability is vacuous. Finally, we provide an epistemic foundation: rationalizability is behaviorally implied by pairwise rationality and common belief in pairwise rationality, while conjecture-rationalizable stability additionally requires belief correctness.

processed.summary:
Introduces **conjecture-rationalizable stability (CRS)**, a new solution concept for matching markets with **externalities** that ensures a stable matching always exists.
 Employs **rationalizable conjectures** based on iterated elimination of beliefs, extending the classical **Gale-Shapley** stability model to scenarios where agents care about others' matches.
 Provides a robust framework for **matching with couples**, yielding predictive results in cases where standard stability concepts would be vacuous.
 Establishes a formal **epistemic foundation**, linking rationalizability to common belief in pairwise rationality and belief correctness.
key_results: Existence proof: Conjecture-rationalizable stable matchings are guaranteed to exist in any finite market.
Compatibility: The model reduces to Gale-Shapley stability in the absence of externalities.
Non-empty predictions: Successfully resolves matching-with-couples problems where standard stable sets are empty.
Equilibrium link: Shows that every conjecture-rationalizable stable matching is a rationalizable matching.
Behavioral mapping: Defines rationalizability as a consequence of common belief in pairwise rationality.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers define conjecture-rationalizable stability to resolve matching problems with externalities where traditional Gale-Shapley stability fails.

title: Indecision and accuracy under social information across groups sizes
summary: Observing the decisions and actions of others provides social information that can inform decisions such as whether to follow. We consider a model where agents simultaneously gather stochastic private information, each deciding once sufficiently confident. Observed decisions and indecision provide social information that triggers discrete waves of collective response: a first decision causes others to update and potentially follow, whose decisions in turn provide further social information, generating successive waves. We explore this model across a range of group sizes and report three main findings. First, social information leads to faster and more accurate decisions than individual decision-making, but agent-level accuracy is maximised at a finite optimal group size. This contrasts with the accuracy of the majority choice, which increases monotonically with the number of agents. Second, waves frequently fail to resolve collective indecision, particularly for smaller groups and when the first decision is incorrect, leaving a subgroup of agents unconvinced. Third, these remaining undecided agents are systematically biased and make less accurate subsequent decisions, with this inaccuracy growing with group size.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **stochastic model** for collective decision-making where agents process private information and observe peer **indecision** as a social signal.
• Identifies that social information triggers **discrete waves** of response, where a first mover's action forces a Bayesian update across the entire group.
• Demonstrates a trade-off where **majority choice accuracy** increases with group size, but **individual agent accuracy** peaks and then declines.
• Evaluates the phenomenon of **persistent indecision**, finding that agents who fail to decide during initial waves become increasingly biased and less accurate.
key_results: Social information leads to faster and more accurate decisions than isolated individual processing.
Individual agent-level accuracy is maximized at a finite optimal group size rather than scaling indefinitely.
The accuracy of the majority choice increases monotonically with the total number of agents.
Waves of social information frequently fail to resolve indecision in small groups or when the first decision is incorrect.
Remaining undecided agents after collective waves show systematic bias and decreased accuracy that worsens with group size.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv research shows that while social information improves collective accuracy, individual agent performance peaks at a specific group size.

title: TempAct: Advancing Temporal Plausibility in Autoregressive Video Generation via Planner-Executor RL
summary: Autoregressive (AR) video diffusion models enable low-latency streaming generation by synthesizing videos chunk by chunk with cached visual context, but this chunk-wise formulation makes temporal instruction following ambiguous. A single global prompt does not specify which sub-event should be realized in each chunk, while naively switching to step-wise prompts often leads to delayed reactions, blended step semantics, and error propagation across prompt transitions. These failures are difficult to address with supervised fine-tuning or distillation alone: SFT suffers from exposure bias, while rollout-based distillation still optimizes low-level denoising or teacher-distribution matching rather than directly enforcing action ordering and prompt-transition correctness. We address these challenges with TempAct, a planner--executor reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes temporal decomposition and step-conditioned execution for temporally plausible AR video generation. TempAct uses an LLM planner to explore span-aware step prompts that are executable by the video model, and trains an AR diffusion executor to follow these prompts under its own generated histories. Its key mechanism is hierarchical group exploration: candidate plans form planning groups, and each plan induces an execution group of multiple continuations from a shared visual context, enabling plan-level credit assignment for long-horizon temporal outcomes and executor-level credit assignment for prompt-switch behavior. We further design hierarchical rewards that combine plan-quality and full-video temporal feedback for the planner with local transition-level step-following rewards, aesthetic regularization, and KL constraints for the executor. Experiments on Self-Forcing and LongLive show that TempAct improves temporal consistency while preserving overall visual quality.

processed.summary:
• **TempAct** introduces a hierarchical **planner-executor RL framework** designed to resolve temporal ambiguity and instruction-following errors in chunk-wise autoregressive (AR) video generation.
• The architecture employs an **LLM-based planner** to decompose global prompts into span-aware sub-events, while an **AR diffusion executor** is trained to follow these transitions using its own generated visual context.
• A novel **hierarchical group exploration** mechanism enables precise credit assignment, optimizing both high-level plan quality and low-level transition-level step-following through specific reward functions.
• This approach successfully mitigates **exposure bias** and semantic blending issues common in supervised fine-tuning, ensuring long-horizon temporal plausibility and consistency.
key_results: Significant improvement in temporal consistency and plausibility for long-duration video synthesis.
Reduction in semantic blending and delayed reaction errors during prompt-switch transitions.
Effective mitigation of exposure bias by training the executor under its own generated histories via RL.
Demonstrated superior performance on the Self-Forcing and LongLive benchmarks compared to SFT and distillation.
Maintained high aesthetic and visual quality while strictly enforcing action ordering and timing.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: TempAct researchers develop a hierarchical RL framework that synchronizes LLM planning with video diffusion to ensure temporally consistent video generation.

title: The Signal-Coverage Matrix: Stratifying Type and Semantic Errors in Statement Autoformalization
summary: Headline type-correctness (TC\%) of LLM autoformalization has climbed from $\sim$53\% to $\sim$76\% in two years, yet this scalar conceals which errors each method resolves. We propose a signal-coverage matrix that crosses the Lean elaborator (pass/fail) with a semantic-equivalence judgment (equivalent/not), sorting every output into one of four cells: true success (TS), type-only (TO), semantic-only (SO), or both fail (BF). On ProofNet\# and MiniF2F-test with DeepSeek V4-Pro across Vanilla, Lean-Retry, Sample-Filter, and Stratified Autoformalization (SAF): (1) the +34 to +36 TS gain across the three elab-feedback methods is $\sim$64\% type-stratum recovery, with SO flat on net (87.5\% of original semantic errors rescued, 8 newly created). (2) The TO-to-TS rate is 23/61 for each method (Wilson 95\% CI [26.6\%, 50.3\%]), and this stratum-level recovery rate predicts $Δ$TS on held-out methods to within 2/186 and renders $Δ$TC linear in the Vanilla elab-fail rate across six (model, dataset) cells ($R^2=0.96$). (3) The two judges disagree by 26 to 37 pp on elab-feedback outputs (vs. 7 pp on Vanilla), with 30 to 56\% of symbolic-judge false negatives traceable to elaborator-forced rewrites. The persistent residual reduces to two gold-formalization errors. TC\% gains should be credited by which cell moved, not by the scalar alone.

processed.summary:
• Proposes the **Signal-Coverage Matrix**, a new diagnostic framework that categorizes LLM autoformalization outputs into four distinct quadrants based on **type-correctness** and **semantic equivalence**.
• Analyzes the impact of **elaborator-feedback loops** (e.g., Lean-Retry), revealing that while they significantly boost type-correctness, they primarily resolve syntax errors rather than semantic ones.
• Establishes a **stratum-level recovery rate** that allows researchers to predict the performance of autoformalization methods on unseen datasets with high linear precision.
• Critiques the industry's reliance on **scalar metrics**, demonstrating how a singular percentage can mask the difference between syntactically valid code and mathematically correct logic.
key_results: Feedback-driven methods achieved a 34% to 36% gain in True Success by recovering approximately 64% of type-stratum errors.
The Type-Only to True Success (TO-to-TS) recovery rate was found to be constant at 23/61 across multiple tested methods.
Scalar Type-Correctness gains are highly linear (R-squared of 0.96) relative to the initial failure rate of the model.
Symbolic judges and human evaluators disagree by up to 37 percentage points on feedback-generated outputs due to forced rewrites.
Identified that remaining errors in high-performing models like DeepSeek V4-Pro are often due to gold-standard formalization mistakes.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce the Signal-Coverage Matrix to reveal that current autoformalization gains prioritize syntax over semantic mathematical truth.

title: From Detection to Action: Using LLM Agents for Fault-Tolerant Control
summary: We propose an agentic Large Language Model (LLM) framework for active Fault-Tolerant Control (FTC) that transforms fault detection outputs into constraint-aware recovery actions grounded in plant-specific knowledge.
